proposals.title: Deciphering the Metastatic Niche Epigenome: A Multi-Site Analysis of Organ-Specific DNA Methylation and Chromatin States in Matched Primary and Disseminated Cancers
proposals.motivation: Metastasis is the primary cause of cancer mortality, yet the epigenetic basis for organ-specific colonization remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies, including those from our group, have largely focused on epigenetic alterations in primary tumors and their association with relapse, leaving a critical gap in understanding whether metastatic sites establish unique, site-adaptive epigenetic programs that drive survival and therapy resistance in distant organs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metastatic cells establish distinct, organ-specific epigenetic landscapes—particularly in DNA methylation and histone modifications—that are not present in the primary tumor and are essential for adaptation and proliferation within the secondary niche.
proposals.method: We will perform multi-omics profiling on matched patient samples (primary tumor and multiple metastatic sites) from cohorts of lung and colorectal cancer. Using high-throughput DNA methylation arrays (Infinium EPIC) and chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq for H3K4me3, H3K27ac, H3K27me3), we will identify site-specific epigenetic alterations. Data will be integrated using statistical models (linear mixed-effects models) to control for patient-specific effects, and machine learning (random forest classifiers) will be used to define metastasis-specific epigenetic signatures predictive of organotropism and clinical outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically map the epigenetic divergence between primary and metastatic tumors, identifying novel, niche-specific epigenetic drivers of metastasis. It will provide a resource of potential epigenetic vulnerabilities specific to metastatic sites, offering new targets for therapies aimed at preventing or treating disseminated disease.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the prevailing view of metastatic epigenetics as a mere extension of the primary tumor by directly testing for *de novo*, site-of-dissemination-specific programs, a systematic multi-site analysis largely absent from pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Mapping the Dynamic Epigenetic Landscape of Transient Drug Tolerance and Relapse in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in oncology is acquired resistance to targeted therapies, often leading to relapse. Pre-2026 studies, including our own prognostic models in NSCLC, largely treat epigenetic alterations as stable drivers of resistance. However, clinical observations of transient drug tolerance suggest the existence of dynamic, reversible epigenetic adaptations that are not captured by current models, representing a critical knowledge gap in understanding and preventing relapse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of cancer cells survives initial therapy through rapid, reversible epigenetic adaptations—a 'phenotypic plasticity' state—that is distinct from the stable epigenetic alterations associated with permanent resistance, and that these dynamic changes can be mapped and linked to subsequent relapse.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived NSCLC cell lines and longitudinal patient plasma samples (ctDNA) pre-, during, and post-tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy. Dynamic epigenetic profiling will be performed via serial, high-depth whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) and ChIP-seq for H3K4me3/H3K27me3 bivalent marks at multiple timepoints. We will employ statistical models, including hidden Markov models (HMMs), to distinguish stable from transient epigenetic alterations. Machine learning classifiers (e.g., random forests) will integrate these dynamic epigenetic signatures with transcriptomic data to predict relapse trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution map of dynamic epigenetic changes during therapy-induced stress in NSCLC. It will identify novel, transient epigenetic biomarkers of drug tolerance that precede stable resistance and relapse, offering new targets for therapeutic interventions aimed at blocking the plasticity state and improving long-term patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly challenges the prevailing assumption of epigenetic stability in resistance by focusing on its dynamic, reversible component—a concept underexplored in solid tumors like NSCLC. The integration of longitudinal epigenetic profiling with computational modeling of relapse prediction represents a novel systems-level approach to understanding therapeutic failure.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Epigenetic Landscape of Single Genetic Clones in Cancer: A Driver of Relapse and Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Intra-tumor heterogeneity is a major obstacle in cancer treatment, driving therapeutic resistance and relapse. While genetic clonal architecture is a recognized contributor, the extent and functional impact of epigenetic variation within a single, defined genetic clone remains poorly characterized. Determining whether epigenetic states are merely passengers of genetic clones or independent drivers of phenotypic diversity is critical for understanding tumor evolution and developing more effective therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a single, stable genetic clone, extensive and functionally significant epigenetic heterogeneity exists, and that this non-genetic variation is a primary driver of phenotypic plasticity, therapeutic response, and relapse potential in cancer.
proposals.method: We will employ patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) to isolate pure, stable genetic clones via single-cell DNA sequencing and barcoding. From these clones, we will perform high-depth, single-cell multi-omics analysis (scATAC-seq and scRNA-seq) on hundreds of cells per clone to map chromatin accessibility and transcriptomic states. Data will be integrated using established computational pipelines (e.g., ArchR, Seurat) to define epigenetic subpopulations. Functional validation will involve isolating epigenetic subpopulations by FACS based on accessible chromatin regions and testing their drug response and tumorigenic potential in vitro and in vivo.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of epigenetic variation constrained within a single cancer genetic clone, directly testing the primacy of clonal architecture. It will identify specific epigenetic alterations associated with relapse-related phenotypes, such as drug-tolerant persister states. The findings will refine models of tumor evolution and highlight non-genetic therapeutic targets to combat heterogeneity.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies correlate bulk epigenetics with genetics or examine epigenetic heterogeneity in mixed populations. Our approach is novel by enforcing a strict isogenic context via clonal isolation, allowing us to unequivocally attribute observed epigenetic and phenotypic diversity to non-genetic mechanisms, a critical and unresolved question in the field.

proposals.title: Mapping Non-Linear Epigenetic State Transitions in Cancer: A Network Analysis of Differentiation Chaos
proposals.motivation: The classical model of cellular differentiation is linear and hierarchical, but cancer progression and relapse are often characterized by phenotypic plasticity and chaotic state transitions. Pre-2026 epigenomic studies, including those from our lab, have cataloged alterations in DNA methylation and histone modifications associated with tumor subtypes, yet they largely assume ordered progression. This fails to explain the rapid, adaptive shifts in tumor cell identity that drive therapy resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that epigenetic reprogramming in advanced tumors follows non-linear, network-like dynamics rather than a linear hierarchy, and that specific chromatin activity states act as hubs facilitating chaotic transitions between differentiation programs.
proposals.method: We will perform multi-omics profiling (whole-genome bisulfite sequencing, ChIP-seq for H3K27ac/H3K4me3, RNA-seq) on longitudinal patient-derived samples from non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) at diagnosis and relapse. Using these data, we will construct epigenetic state transition networks. Each node will represent a unique combinatorial chromatin/DNA methylation state, and edges will be weighted by transition probabilities inferred from single-cell methylation (scBS-seq) and ATAC-seq data from paired samples. We will apply graph theory metrics (e.g., centrality, modularity) and non-linear dimensionality reduction (PHATE) to identify chaotic attractors and hub states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework to model epigenetic plasticity in cancer, moving beyond linear models. It will identify key chromatin hubs that mediate chaotic state transitions, offering new targets to lock tumors in a therapy-sensitive state and prevent relapse.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant linear differentiation paradigm by applying network theory to epigenomic data, explicitly testing for chaotic dynamics. It integrates longitudinal clinical relapse data with multi-layer epigenomics to map real-world state transitions in patient tumors.

proposals.title: Longitudinal single-cell chromatin accessibility profiling reveals the epigenetic dynamics of acquired therapy resistance in non-small cell lung cancer
proposals.motivation: The emergence of therapy resistance is a major cause of cancer relapse, but its underlying mechanisms are often heterogeneous and poorly understood at the cellular level. Pre-2026, bulk epigenomic analyses have identified alterations associated with resistance, but they mask the diversity and evolution of individual cell states within tumors. Applying single-cell epigenetic assays longitudinally to clinical samples is critical to dissect this complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal tracking of single-cell chromatin accessibility in patient biopsies will reveal recurrent, cell-type-specific epigenetic programs and regulatory trajectories that drive the acquisition of therapy resistance in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell ATAC-seq on serial tumor biopsies from NSCLC patients before treatment, at initial response, and upon clinical relapse. Samples will be processed using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform (pre-2026 standard). Data analysis will involve clustering (Seurat, ArchR) to define cell states, trajectory inference (Monocle3, Slingshot) to model epigenetic evolution, and identification of transcription factor motifs and cis-regulatory elements associated with resistant states. Findings will be integrated with matched genomic and transcriptomic data where available.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution map of epigenetic evolution during therapy failure in a clinical setting. It will identify novel, non-genetic drivers of resistance and define epigenetic biomarkers predictive of relapse. The data will serve as a resource for developing epigenetic combination therapies to prevent or overcome resistance.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell epigenomics has been applied to cancer, its longitudinal use on matched patient biopsies over a treatment course is novel. This moves beyond static snapshots to directly observe the dynamic epigenetic rewiring that constitutes a cell's 'memory' of therapy exposure and escape.

proposals.title: CRISPR Lineage Tracing and Epigenomic Profiling Reveal the Clonal Dynamics of Epigenetic Escape in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: Cancer relapse is driven by the selection of resistant clones, but the temporal relationship between genetic alterations and the epigenetic evolution enabling this survival remains poorly mapped. Pre-2026 studies by Esteller and others have shown that epigenetic alterations are associated with cancer progression and therapy resistance, yet we lack tools to longitudinally track how epigenetic states evolve within defined cellular lineages during treatment and relapse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relapse of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) following therapy is driven by specific, heritable epigenetic reprogramming events within genetically defined subclones, which can be captured by coupling cellular barcoding with epigenomic analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ a CRISPR-Cas9-based cellular barcoding system (e.g., using a poly-GT barcode integrated into a safe-harbor locus) to uniquely label tumor cells in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of NSCLC. Barcoded populations will be subjected to standard-of-care therapy (e.g., cisplatin/pemetrexed) and monitored for relapse. At serial timepoints (pre-treatment, during regression, at relapse), we will perform single-cell RNA/DNA sequencing to reconstruct clonal lineages from barcodes and associate them with transcriptional states. Subsequently, we will use bulk-cell sorting of dominant relapse-associated barcoded clones for integrative multi-omics analysis, including whole-genome bisulfite sequencing and ChIP-seq for H3K27ac and H3K4me3, to identify clone-specific epigenetic alterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for directly linking clonal lineage to epigenetic evolution in cancer. It will identify the specific epigenetic pathways co-opted by relapse-driving clones, moving beyond associative studies to causal lineage models. The findings could reveal new epigenetic biomarkers or therapeutic targets to prevent relapse by pre-emptively eliminating epigenetically primed subpopulations.
proposals.novelty: While lineage tracing is established in development and barcoding has been used to track genetic clonal dynamics in cancer, its direct integration with deep epigenomic profiling to dissect the epigenetic life history of relapse clones is novel. This approach transcends correlative snapshots to establish a temporal and causal map of epigenetic escape.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Epigenetic Profiling of Histone Modifications by Mass Cytometry Identifies Heterogeneity and Predicts Relapse in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: Tumor heterogeneity is a major driver of therapy resistance and relapse in cancer, yet bulk epigenomic analyses mask this cellular diversity. Pre-2026, the role of combinatorial histone modification patterns in single cells, particularly within the tumor microenvironment, remains poorly characterized in solid tumors like non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). A high-dimensional, single-cell tool is needed to link epigenetic states directly to clinical outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, co-occurring histone modification signatures, measurable at single-cell resolution in NSCLC biopsies, define epigenetically distinct subpopulations of tumor and stromal cells that are associated with disease progression and risk of relapse.
proposals.method: We will adapt mass cytometry (CyTOF) using a panel of ~15 metal-tagged antibodies targeting key activating (H3K4me3, H3K27ac) and repressive (H3K9me3, H3K27me3) histone marks, alongside lineage markers (EpCAM, CD45) and signaling proteins. Single-cell suspensions from primary NSCLC patient samples (n=50) with longitudinal clinical data will be stained, acquired on a Helios CyTOF, and analyzed. We will employ dimensionality reduction (viSNE, UMAP) and unsupervised clustering (PhenoGraph) to identify epigenetic subpopulations and correlate their abundance with relapse-free survival using Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, high-parameter workflow for single-cell epigenetic phenotyping in solid tumors. It will directly link specific histone modification-based cell states to clinical relapse in NSCLC, moving beyond bulk DNA methylation analyses and providing a functional, cellular map of epigenetic heterogeneity with prognostic value.
proposals.novelty: While mass cytometry is established for immunophenotyping, its application to systematically profile a broad panel of histone modifications in archived human tumor tissue at single-cell resolution is novel. This approach moves beyond assessing single marks in bulk to decode the combinatorial epigenetic code within the tumor ecosystem.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Metabolic Flux Modeling of Methyl and Acetyl Group Dynamics to Predict Epigenetic Heterogeneity and Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: Epigenetic alterations, particularly in DNA methylation and histone acetylation, are central to cancer initiation, progression, and relapse. However, current static snapshots of epigenetic marks fail to capture the dynamic metabolic processes that supply the methyl and acetyl groups, limiting our understanding of intra-tumor heterogeneity and therapeutic resistance. Integrating the dynamics of metabolite availability with epigenetic regulation is a critical unmet need in cancer biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying metabolic flux analysis (MFA) principles to model the dynamics of S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) and acetyl-CoA metabolism in single cells will reveal flux states that predict epigenetic heterogeneity, cellular plasticity, and the risk of disease relapse in cancer patients.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework adapting constraint-based MFA (e.g., flux balance analysis) to model the production, consumption, and interconversion of key epigenetic metabolites (SAM, acetyl-CoA, α-ketoglutarate) in single cells. This will integrate single-cell RNA-seq data (to infer enzyme expression levels as constraints) with mass spectrometry-based metabolomics from sorted cell populations. The model will be trained and validated on longitudinal samples from non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients, correlating predicted metabolic flux states with single-cell epigenomic profiles (scATAC-seq, scChIP-seq) and clinical relapse data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative link between metabolic flux dynamics and epigenetic regulation at single-cell resolution. It will provide a novel predictive model for identifying epigenetic plasticity states associated with cancer relapse, offering new biomarkers and potential metabolic targets to prevent therapeutic failure.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic and epigenetic studies are often separate, this proposal uniquely applies formal metabolic flux modeling, a staple of systems biology, to the dynamic problem of epigenetic substrate supply, moving beyond correlative associations to mechanistic, predictive models of epigenetic fate in cancer.

proposals.title: Decoding the Spatial Niche: Predicting Cellular Microenvironment from Single-Cell DNA Methylation Profiles in Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a critical determinant of cancer progression and therapy response. While spatial transcriptomics can map cellular niches, it is costly and not routinely performed. Conversely, single-cell epigenomic assays, particularly DNA methylation, are becoming widespread for profiling cellular states and identifying epigenetic alterations associated with relapse. A method to infer a cell's spatial context and interactions directly from its epigenetic profile would unlock microenvironmental insights from vast existing and future single-cell epigenomic datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the epigenetic state of a cancer or stromal cell, shaped by persistent signaling from its specific niche, contains a stable, readable signature that can be used to predict its spatial microenvironmental context, including major cellular interaction partners and niche type (e.g., immune-rich, hypoxic, invasive front).
proposals.method: We will leverage paired single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) and single-cell DNA methylation (scBS-seq or scEM-seq) data from dissociated tumors to train a model. First, we will use scRNA-seq deconvolution methods (e.g., CIBERSORTx) on bulk spatial transcriptomic data (Visium) to assign probable spatial niches to individual cells in the paired single-cell data. These niche labels will serve as ground truth. We will then train a supervised deep learning model (a convolutional neural network on methylation windows or a graph neural network on chromatin interaction maps derived from ATAC-seq) to predict the niche label from the single-cell epigenetic profile. Validation will be performed on independent datasets and via immunohistochemistry on serial sections.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational framework, 'EpiToNiche', for inferring spatial context from single-cell epigenomes. It will provide a new tool to retrospectively analyze the TME from epigenetic biobanks and link epigenetic alterations directly to microenvironmental forces. This could reveal new mechanisms of therapy resistance and identify epigenetic biomarkers of high-risk spatial niches.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate bulk epigenetics with microenvironment features, predicting the spatial niche of an individual cell from its epigenetic profile alone is an unsolved inverse problem. This approach moves beyond correlating cell types to actively reconstructing interaction networks, using DNA methylation's stability as a historical record of microenvironmental exposure.

proposals.title: Systematic functional dissection of epitranscriptomic marks in cancer: Distinguishing driver modifications from passenger events
proposals.motivation: The epitranscriptome, particularly N6-methyladenosine (m6A), is widely implicated in cancer progression and relapse, with alterations in writers, erasers, and readers frequently observed. However, the field predominantly reports associations, leaving a critical gap: it is unknown whether all detected RNA modifications are functionally regulatory in tumorigenesis or if some are merely passive bystanders (passenger events) with no oncogenic consequence. Distinguishing between these possibilities is essential for prioritizing therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant subset of RNA modifications detected in cancer cells and patient samples are passenger events, not causally linked to tumorigenic phenotypes, and that their functional impact can be disentangled through integrated genomic and epigenomic analysis.
proposals.method: We will perform multi-omics profiling (MeRIP-seq for m6A, RNA-seq, and small RNA-seq for miRNAs) on a panel of patient-derived cancer cell lines and matched primary tumor/normal tissues. Using statistical models akin to those in prognostic prediction analyses (e.g., LASSO regression, survival analysis), we will correlate modification landscapes with gene expression, miRNA abundance, and chromatin accessibility data (ATAC-seq). CRISPR-Cas9 screens will then be used to functionally validate candidate 'regulatory' vs. 'bystander' modification sites by targeting associated writer enzymes and assessing impacts on proliferation, invasion, and therapy resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional map categorizing RNA modifications in cancer as drivers or passengers, directly testing a core assumption of the epitranscriptomics field. It will yield a prioritized list of therapeutically relevant epitranscriptomic targets and establish a rigorous framework for evaluating their role in cancer relapse and progression.
proposals.novelty: While most studies assume all modifications are functional, this proposal explicitly tests the null hypothesis that many are bystanders, applying a functional genomics lens reminiscent of the 'driver vs. passenger' mutation paradigm in cancer genomics to the epitranscriptome.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Pro-Tumorigenic Side Effects from Pharmacological Inhibition of RNA Methylation Writers and Erasers in Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic targeting of epigenetic 'writers' and 'erasers' in cancer, particularly DNA methyltransferases and histone deacetylases, is established. However, the analogous targeting of RNA modifiers (e.g., METTL3, FTO, ALKBH5) is a burgeoning field with the assumption that inhibiting oncogenic RNA modifications is uniformly beneficial. Pre-2026 evidence suggests RNA modifications have complex, context-dependent roles in cellular differentiation and immune signaling, raising the critical question of whether their inhibition could inadvertently promote tumorigenic processes like metastasis or immune evasion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pharmacological inhibition of specific RNA methylation writers (e.g., METTL3) or erasers (e.g., FTO) will, in defined cellular contexts and cancer subtypes, activate pro-tumorigenic transcriptional programs and cellular phenotypes, potentially mediated through altered chromatin accessibility and stress response pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-omics approach in a panel of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and leukemia cell lines treated with selective METTL3 and FTO inhibitors. Phenotypic consequences on invasion, colony formation, and drug tolerance will be quantified. Integrated analysis will combine RNA-seq, meRIP-seq (for m6A mapping), and ATAC-seq (for chromatin accessibility) from treated vs. control cells. Statistical evaluation of prognostic signatures and pathway enrichment (using algorithms like GSVA and GSEA) will link inhibitor-induced alterations to known poor-prognosis gene sets. Validation will involve CRISPRi rescue experiments and analysis of patient-derived xenograft models for relapse-associated alterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of potential adverse effects from inhibiting RNA modifiers in cancer, moving the field beyond a purely oncogene-centric view. It will identify specific contexts (cancer type, genetic background) where caution is warranted, informing combination therapy strategies and patient stratification for future clinical trials targeting the epitranscriptome.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 research focuses on the anti-tumor efficacy of RNA modifier inhibition, this proposal directly investigates its potential iatrogenic, pro-tumorigenic side effects—a critical but largely overlooked dimension for therapeutic development. It uniquely integrates epitranscriptomic profiling with chromatin activity readouts to uncover mechanistic links.

proposals.title: Systematic Analysis of Somatic Mutations in RNA-Modifying Proteins as Drivers of Cancer Progression and Relapse
proposals.motivation: The epitranscriptome, governed by writers, readers, and erasers of RNA modifications, is a critical layer of gene regulation. Pre-2026 studies have shown that specific enzymes like METTL3 or FTO can act as oncogenes, but a comprehensive, pan-cancer analysis of genetic alterations across the entire RNA-modifying machinery and their association with clinical outcomes is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of their role in tumor evolution and relapse, a key theme in Dr. Esteller's recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, recurrent somatic mutations in RNA-modifying proteins are not passenger events but are positively selected, driving cancer biology by disrupting the epitranscriptome, and that these mutational signatures are associated with distinct epigenetic states and poor prognosis, including disease relapse.
proposals.method: We will perform an integrated multi-omics analysis using pre-2026 TCGA and other public cohorts. First, we will catalog all somatic mutations in a curated list of RNA-modifying proteins and identify significantly mutated genes using MutSig2CV. We will then correlate these mutational landscapes with matched DNA methylation (Infinium EPIC array) and chromatin accessibility (ATAC-seq) data to identify associated epigenetic alterations. Prognostic models will be built using Cox proportional-hazards regression, with a focus on relapse-free survival, and validated in independent datasets.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic map linking genetic disruption of the epitranscriptomic machinery to epigenetic dysregulation and clinical outcomes across cancer types. It will identify novel candidate driver mutations in RNA-modifying proteins and define their associated 'epitranscriptomic-epigenetic' signatures, offering new biomarkers for prognosis and potential therapeutic targets to prevent relapse.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focused on expression or single enzymes, this proposal uniquely integrates genetic mutation data with epigenetic states to mechanistically link alterations in RNA-modifying proteins to cancer biology, moving beyond correlation to identify causal regulatory networks that may explain therapeutic failure and relapse.

proposals.title: Epigenetic Silencing of RNA Methylation Regulators as a Therapeutic Strategy to Prevent Relapse in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: Aberrant RNA modifications, particularly N6-methyladenosine (m6A), are established drivers of tumor progression and therapy resistance in cancer, including non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). The dynamic expression of m6A writer (e.g., METTL3) and eraser (e.g., FTO) enzymes is associated with poor prognosis and relapse, but directly targeting their protein function remains challenging. Pre-2026 epigenetic editing tools offer a novel avenue to durably modulate the expression of these key regulatory genes at the transcriptional level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CRISPR-based epigenetic silencing of specific RNA modifier genes (writers/erasers) will durably reprogram the epitranscriptome of NSCLC cells, leading to a reversal of oncogenic gene expression programs and a reduction in tumor relapse potential.
proposals.method: We will employ a CRISPR-dCas9 system fused to the KRAB transcriptional repressor domain (CRISPRi) to epigenetically silence METTL3 and FTO in a panel of patient-derived NSCLC cell lines. Using sgRNAs targeting the promoters of these genes, we will establish stable epigenetic repression and validate knockdown via qPCR and western blot. Functional outcomes will be assessed through m6A-seq, RNA-seq, and in vitro assays for proliferation, invasion, and drug sensitivity. The therapeutic potential will be tested in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models, monitoring for tumor growth and relapse post-chemotherapy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for using epigenetic editing to target the cancer epitranscriptome, a largely unexplored therapeutic axis. It will directly link the transcriptional control of RNA modifier genes to durable phenotypic changes in tumor aggressiveness and relapse, providing a novel experimental framework for cancer therapy that complements existing genetic and pharmacological approaches.
proposals.novelty: While the role of m6A in cancer is known, using CRISPR-based transcriptional control to durably modulate the expression of RNA modifier enzymes, rather than their catalytic activity, is a novel strategy. This approach merges the fields of epigenetic editing and epitranscriptomics to address cancer relapse, a core theme in Dr. Esteller's recent work on prognostic models and chromatin biology.

proposals.title: Global Profiling of RNA Epitranscriptomic Modifications in Cancer Relapse Using Mass Spectrometry-Based Proteomic Workflows
proposals.motivation: The epitranscriptome, comprising chemical modifications to RNA, is a crucial regulator of gene expression and cellular identity, with established roles in cancer progression. However, systematic, quantitative analysis of global RNA modification changes, particularly in the context of cancer relapse and therapy resistance, remains technically challenging. Adapting robust, high-throughput proteomic mass spectrometry (MS) pipelines offers a powerful, untapped approach to quantify these modifications across patient samples.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cancer relapse is associated with specific, quantifiable alterations in the global RNA modification landscape, and that these epitranscriptomic signatures can serve as prognostic biomarkers and reveal novel therapeutic vulnerabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) workflows, optimized for post-translational modifications, to analyze hydrolyzed RNA nucleosides from patient-derived cancer cells and matched primary/relapse tumor tissues. RNA will be extracted, digested to nucleosides, and analyzed using a high-resolution Q Exactive HF-X mass spectrometer. Data-independent acquisition (DIA) will be employed for unbiased quantification. Bioinformatics pipelines, including MaxQuant for feature detection and alignment, will be customized to identify and quantify known (e.g., m6A, m5C, pseudouridine) and novel modifications, with statistical analysis linking modification levels to clinical relapse data.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a robust MS-based pipeline for the global quantification of the RNA epitranscriptome in clinical samples. It will directly link specific RNA modification profiles to cancer relapse, identifying novel prognostic biomarkers. Furthermore, it will provide a foundational dataset for understanding how epitranscriptomic alterations drive therapeutic resistance, opening new avenues for intervention.
proposals.novelty: While MS is standard in proteomics, its systematic application for global, quantitative RNA modification profiling in matched primary and relapse cancer patient samples is novel. This approach moves beyond candidate-gene studies (e.g., focusing on METTL3 or FTO) to an unbiased, systems-level view of the epitranscriptome's role in disease progression.

proposals.title: Epitranscriptomic Erosion: Mapping the Loss of m6A RNA Methylation as a Driver of Acquired Resistance to Targeted Cancer Therapies
proposals.motivation: The dynamic landscape of RNA modifications, particularly N6-methyladenosine (m6A), is a crucial regulator of gene expression in cancer. While the establishment of these marks is studied, the systematic erasure of therapeutic RNA modifications during treatment remains unexplored. Understanding this 'epitranscriptomic erosion' is vital, as it may represent a novel, reversible mechanism of acquired drug resistance, a major clinical challenge in oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prolonged exposure to targeted anticancer therapies induces the selective loss of m6A modifications on key transcripts involved in drug response and cell survival, and that this erasure, mediated by altered activity of 'eraser' proteins (e.g., FTO, ALKBH5), directly drives the relapse of cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will employ patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) treated until relapse with EGFR or ALK inhibitors. Using pre-2026 gold-standard techniques, we will perform longitudinal m6A-seq and RNA-seq on sensitive and resistant tumors. Differential m6A peak analysis will identify sites of modification loss. Integrative bioinformatics, including gene set enrichment analysis and correlation with chromatin accessibility data (ATAC-seq), will link erasure events to altered signaling pathways. Functional validation will use CRISPRi/a to modulate eraser enzyme activity in resistant cell lines, assessing drug sensitivity restoration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between RNA modification erasure and therapy resistance, providing a new layer of epigenetic regulation in cancer relapse. It will identify a novel set of predictive biomarkers (specific m6A loss sites) and propose 'eraser' enzymes as potential therapeutic targets to re-sensitize resistant tumors, moving beyond DNA-centric epigenetic therapies.
proposals.novelty: While most epitranscriptomic research focuses on writer-mediated m6A deposition, this proposal uniquely investigates the pathological consequences of its removal. It directly connects the dynamic loss of a specific RNA modification to a concrete clinical outcome—acquired drug resistance—in a longitudinal, therapy-relevant model.

proposals.title: Epitranscriptomic Hypermodification as a Novel Therapeutic Strategy to Induce Synthetic Lethality in Chemotherapy-Resistant Cancers
proposals.motivation: The dynamic landscape of RNA modifications (the epitranscriptome) is a key regulator of gene expression and cell fate, with specific alterations strongly associated with cancer progression and therapy resistance. However, current therapeutic strategies targeting these modifications are largely inhibitory. A proactive, synthetic approach to engineer global RNA hypermodification represents an unexplored avenue to disrupt the precise epitranscriptomic balance required for cancer cell survival, particularly in relapse-prone malignancies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the artificial, large-scale induction of specific RNA modifications (e.g., m6A, m5C) beyond physiological levels will create an 'epitranscriptomic catastrophe,' disrupting RNA metabolism and proteostasis to induce synthetic lethality in cancer cells, especially those with pre-existing vulnerabilities in DNA damage or metabolic pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ CRISPR/dCas9-based transcriptional activators to upregulate key RNA methyltransferases (e.g., METTL3/14 for m6A, NSUN2 for m5C) in a panel of patient-derived cancer cell lines, including non-small cell lung cancer models with known relapse-associated gene alterations. Hypermodification will be quantified via MeRIP-seq and bisulfite sequencing. Functional impact will be assessed using high-throughput viability assays, ribosome profiling, and polysome analysis. Statistical evaluation of prognostic models will identify baseline epitranscriptomic signatures predictive of hypermodification sensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish 'epitranscriptomic engineering' as a new therapeutic paradigm, moving beyond inhibition to active dysregulation. It will provide a systematic analysis of which cancer subtypes and genetic backgrounds are most vulnerable to this approach, potentially identifying novel biomarkers for patient stratification and new targets for combinatorial therapies to prevent relapse.
proposals.novelty: While the role of epitranscriptomic alterations in cancer is known, the deliberate induction of hypermodification as a therapeutic tool is a novel, non-obvious strategy. It leverages the cancer cell's own dependency on precise RNA regulation as an Achilles' heel, a concept distinct from simply restoring normal modification patterns.

proposals.title: Functional Dissection of T1D-Associated Genetic Variants in Cytokine-Responsive Enhancer Networks of Human Pancreatic Beta Cells
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous non-coding variants associated with Type 1 Diabetes (T1D), many of which are postulated to reside in immune-responsive regulatory elements. However, pre-2026, the functional impact of these variants on enhancer activity in the primary target cell—the pancreatic beta cell—remains largely unvalidated. This gap hinders the transition from genetic association to mechanistic understanding of how genetic predisposition intersects with inflammatory triggers to drive disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific T1D-associated single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) disrupt the binding of key transcription factors (e.g., STATs, NF-κB) within cytokine-responsive enhancers, thereby altering the transcriptional programs in human beta cells that govern their survival and function during insulitis.
proposals.method: We will employ a CRISPR/Cas9-based saturation mutagenesis and reporter assay pipeline in EndoC-βH1 human beta cells. Specific T1D-GWAS SNPs within cytokine-inducible chromatin-accessible regions (identified via pre-2026 ATAC-seq datasets) will be introduced into their native genomic context using homology-directed repair. Enhancer activity will be quantified via a dual-luciferase reporter system and single-cell RNA-sequencing following stimulation with IFN-γ and IL-1β. Statistical evaluation of allelic effects on gene expression will be performed using established algorithms (e.g., DESeq2, MAGENTA) to associate variant alterations with pathway dysregulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct functional validation of high-priority T1D risk variants, moving beyond statistical association to establish causal regulatory mechanisms. It will define a set of 'functional risk variants' and their target genes, offering new insights into the molecular etiology of T1D and nominating novel therapeutic targets aimed at preserving beta cell mass. The study will also deliver a methodological framework for the functional annotation of non-coding variants in other complex diseases.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have mapped chromatin states in beta cells, the direct functional testing of T1D-associated variants within cytokine-responsive enhancers in a physiologically relevant human cell model is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates precise genome editing, cytokine challenge, and single-cell transcriptomics to dissect variant function in the specific pathological context of insulitis.

proposals.title: Single-Enhancer Epigenomic Dynamics in Cancer: Deciphering Transcription Factor Binding and 3D Chromatin Looping at a Key MYC Super-Enhancer in Colorectal Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: Cancer relapse is frequently driven by epigenetic and transcriptional reprogramming, yet our understanding of the fundamental regulatory unit—the individual enhancer—remains coarse. While global analyses link enhancer alterations to poor prognosis, the precise, dynamic mechanisms of transcription factor (TF) binding and chromatin looping at a single, disease-critical enhancer in patient-derived cells are poorly characterized. This gap limits our ability to target enhancer-driven oncogene activation therapeutically.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic, cooperative binding of specific TFs at a key oncogenic super-enhancer, rather than static occupancy, drives aberrant chromatin looping and target gene (e.g., MYC) expression, and that this dynamic architecture is specifically altered in cancer cells from patients who experience relapse.
proposals.method: We will focus on a validated MYC super-enhancer in colorectal cancer. Using patient-derived organoids (from primary and relapsed tumors) and CRISPR/Cas9-mediated tagging, we will apply CUT&RUN for high-resolution TF binding (e.g., AP-1, TCF4) and histone mark mapping. Chromatin looping dynamics will be captured at the single-enhancer level using high-resolution Micro-C and Tri-C techniques. Computational analysis will integrate these datasets using custom pipelines (e.g., HOMER for motif analysis, Mustache for loop calling) to model temporal and cell-state-specific interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational, mechanistic model of how a single enhancer's function is dysregulated in cancer progression and relapse. It will directly link specific TF dynamics to 3D chromatin misfolding in a patient-relevant context, providing a novel framework for understanding epigenetic-driven resistance. The findings could nominate specific, druggable protein-DNA interactions within the enhancer complex.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond associative, genome-wide epigenomic studies to a deep, causal dissection of a single enhancer's real-time operation in patient-derived models represents a significant scale-down in focus with a proportional increase in mechanistic resolution, which is non-obvious from bulk correlation studies.

proposals.title: Deciphering the Oncogenic Role of NSUN5-Mediated Translational Reprogramming in Tumor Progression and Immune Evasion
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies by Esteller and others have established that loss of the RNA methyltransferase NSUN5 triggers a specific translational program in cancer cells, often linked to stress adaptation. However, it remains unknown whether this program is merely a passive stress response or actively drives aggressive tumor phenotypes like invasion and immune escape, which are key determinants of patient relapse and poor prognosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the translational program induced by NSUN5 loss is not solely stress-responsive but actively promotes tumor progression by enhancing invasive potential and enabling evasion from the host immune system.
proposals.method: We will employ isogenic cancer cell models with CRISPR/Cas9-mediated NSUN5 knockout. Using ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq) and polysome RNA-seq, we will identify and validate specific mRNA cohorts undergoing altered translation. Functional assays (3D invasion, co-culture with cytotoxic T-cells) will link these targets to phenotype. We will integrate these data with epigenetic (ChIP-seq for chromatin marks) and transcriptomic profiles from patient cohorts (e.g., TCGA) to identify associated alterations and build prognostic models using statistical algorithms like LASSO-Cox regression, validating their link to relapse.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, active oncogenic mechanism driven by epitranscriptomic loss, moving beyond correlation to causation. It will identify new therapeutic vulnerabilities for NSUN5-deficient tumors and provide a multi-omics signature predictive of invasion and immune evasion, directly informing strategies to prevent relapse.
proposals.novelty: While NSUN5 loss is known to alter translation, its direct functional role in shaping the tumor microenvironment and immune interaction is unexplored. This proposal uniquely challenges the paradigm of a passive adaptive response, testing its active role in two critical cancer hallmarks.

proposals.title: Epitranscriptomic Dysregulation in Cancer: Functional and Structural Consequences of 28S rRNA C3782 Hypomethylation on Ribosome Activity and Translational Fidelity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work has established rRNA methylation as a key epitranscriptomic regulator of ribosome function, with specific alterations linked to cancer. The methylation status of cytosine 3782 in 28S rRNA (C3782) is frequently altered in human tumors, but its precise mechanistic role remains unknown. Determining how this specific modification influences ribosome activity is critical for understanding cancer cell-specific translational programs and identifying novel therapeutic vulnerabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unmethylated state of C3782 in 28S rRNA induces a conformational change in the ribosomal peptidyl transferase center, altering translational fidelity and selectivity for specific mRNA subsets, thereby promoting oncogenic protein synthesis and contributing to cancer cell survival and relapse.
proposals.method: We will employ CRISPR/Cas9-mediated genetic engineering in isogenic cancer cell lines to create models with defined C3782 methylation status. Ribosome structure will be analyzed via cryo-electron microscopy to resolve conformational differences. Functional impact will be assessed through ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq) to quantify translation efficiency and fidelity, and polysome profiling coupled with mass spectrometry to identify altered translational outputs. Statistical evaluation of prognostic models will integrate C3782 status with clinical data from NSCLC patients to assess its association with disease progression and relapse.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link a specific, cancer-associated rRNA epitranscriptomic mark to a defined structural and functional ribosomal alteration. It will identify a novel mechanism of translational control in oncogenesis and provide a potential biomarker for predicting therapeutic response and relapse risk in cancers like NSCLC, based on ribosome epitranscriptomics.
proposals.novelty: While rRNA methylation is known to be dysregulated in cancer, this proposal focuses on a single, poorly characterized site (C3782) and moves beyond correlation to establish direct causal proof of its structural and functional impact, integrating epitranscriptomics, structural biology, and translational profiling in a cancer-specific context.

proposals.title: Systematic Analysis of Non-Canonical DNA Hypermethylation in Immune Evasion and Relapse of Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: While CpG island (CGI) promoter hypermethylation is a well-established epigenetic hallmark of cancer, its role in immune evasion remains incompletely defined. Recent pre-2026 studies suggest that epigenetic alterations in enhancers and non-CGI promoters may be more dynamic and functionally significant in shaping the tumor microenvironment. This project aims to shift focus from CGI promoters to these understudied regulatory elements to identify primary epigenetic drivers of immune escape, a major cause of therapeutic failure and relapse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tumor-specific hypermethylation of enhancers and non-CGI promoters, rather than CGI promoters, is a primary epigenetic mechanism driving the silencing of key immune-related pathways, thereby facilitating immune escape and predicting clinical relapse in NSCLC.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) and integrated chromatin state analysis (H3K27ac, H3K4me1) on a cohort of matched primary and relapsed NSCLC tumors. Differential methylation analysis will be conducted using established pipelines (e.g., methylKit, DSS) with a specific focus on enhancer regions defined by ENCODE/Roadmap Epigenomics and non-CGI promoters. Statistical evaluation of prognostic models will be performed to correlate these non-canonical methylation alterations with immune cell infiltration (from RNA-seq deconvolution using CIBERSORTx) and relapse-free survival.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive map of non-CGI regulatory element hypermethylation linked to immune evasion in NSCLC. It will identify novel epigenetic biomarkers predictive of relapse and reveal new therapeutic targets for reversing immune suppression, moving beyond the traditional CGI-centric view of cancer epigenetics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic shift of focus from CGI promoters to enhancers and non-CGI promoters as primary epigenetic drivers of immune escape, an area largely unexplored in pre-2026 epigenetic research despite its potential mechanistic importance.

proposals.title: Systematic mapping of immune gene dysregulation in early-replicating, hypomethylated domains across cancer types
proposals.motivation: Partially Methylated Domains (PMDs) are well-characterized late-replicating, hypomethylated regions in cancer, but early-replicating domains also exhibit significant methylation loss, a phenomenon less studied. Given that early replication timing is strongly associated with active gene expression and chromatin accessibility, hypomethylation in these regions could have distinct and potent effects on gene regulation, particularly for immune-related genes, influencing tumor immunogenicity and patient relapse. A systematic analysis of this interplay is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that early-replicating domains that undergo cancer-associated hypomethylation are enriched for immune regulatory genes, and that their altered methylation status is a key determinant of immune gene expression dysregulation, contributing to tumor immune evasion and clinical relapse.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-omics data from TCGA and other pre-2026 cohorts. First, we will define early-replicating hypomethylated domains (ER-HMDs) by intersecting replication timing data (Repli-seq) with whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) to identify regions with significant methylation loss compared to normal tissue. We will then perform enrichment analysis for immune gene pathways within ER-HMDs. Statistical evaluation of associations between ER-HMD methylation, gene expression (RNA-seq), and patient relapse will be conducted using Cox proportional hazards models and machine learning (e.g., random forest) to build prognostic signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new epigenetic framework for understanding immune dysregulation in cancer, moving beyond the PMD-centric view. It will identify a novel class of epigenetically dysregulated domains linked to replication timing and provide biomarkers of immune function and relapse risk that could inform immunotherapy strategies.
proposals.novelty: While PMD biology is established, this proposal focuses on the under-explored role of hypomethylation in early-replicating regions. The explicit link between replication timing, domain-specific methylation loss, and immune gene regulation in the context of cancer relapse is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of pre-2026 concepts.

proposals.title: Longitudinal mapping of replication timing-associated DNA hypomethylation as a driver of epigenetic heterogeneity in colorectal cancer progression and relapse
proposals.motivation: Cancer genomes exhibit widespread DNA hypomethylation, a hallmark of malignancy, but the precise mechanisms driving its acquisition during tumor evolution remain unclear. Pre-2026 work suggests late-replicating genomic regions are prone to methylation loss, yet a direct, longitudinal demonstration linking cell division, replication timing, and progressive demethylation in patient-derived models is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how epigenetic heterogeneity arises and contributes to relapse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the number of cell divisions in a tumor lineage, combined with the inherent inefficiency of maintaining methylation in late-replicating regions, directly drives the accumulation of specific hypomethylation alterations, and that these alterations are associated with increased epigenetic instability and relapse risk.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived colorectal cancer organoids subjected to long-term in vitro passaging to simulate tumor evolution. We will perform longitudinal, high-coverage whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) and Repli-seq at multiple passages to track methylation loss dynamics. Alterations will be statistically evaluated using custom algorithms to correlate replication timing (Repli-seq signal) with the rate of methylation loss per division, controlling for genomic context. Identified dynamic hypomethylated regions will be validated in paired primary-relapse patient samples from existing cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative link between replication timing, cell division history, and the acquisition of cancer-specific hypomethylation. It will identify a class of 'replication-fragile' epigenetic loci whose instability may serve as a mitotic clock and a biomarker for tumors prone to relapse, adding a mechanistic layer to prognostic models.
proposals.novelty: While replication timing is known to correlate with methylation patterns, this proposal uniquely tracks its causal role in *de novo* demethylation dynamically during tumor evolution, moving beyond static correlations to a mechanistic, division-counting model in a clinically relevant system.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas9 Screening Identifies Epigenetic Regulators of Immune Evasion and Therapy Relapse in Microsatellite-Unstable Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: Immunotherapy has transformed cancer treatment, yet many patients with immunogenic tumours, such as those with microsatellite instability (MSI), do not respond or eventually relapse. Pre-2026 research, including from our group, has linked epigenetic alterations to immune escape, but a systematic, functional identification of key epigenetic regulators in clinically relevant models is lacking. This gap limits our ability to develop epigenetic combination therapies to overcome resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific epigenetic regulators are essential for cancer cells to evade T-cell-mediated killing, and their genetic disruption will sensitize immunogenic tumours to immune checkpoint blockade, preventing relapse.
proposals.method: We will perform genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screens using the Brunello library in human MSI colorectal cancer organoids co-cultured with autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. Screens will be conducted under selective pressure from anti-PD-1 treatment. Hits will be validated in syngeneic mouse models of MSI cancer. Statistical analysis of screen data will employ the MAGeCK-VISPR algorithm to identify genes whose knockout enriches for sensitivity or resistance to immune-mediated killing. Epigenetic and transcriptomic alterations in validated hits will be characterized by RRBS and RNA-seq.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, functional map of epigenetic dependencies for immune evasion in a high-immunogenicity context. It will nominate novel combinatorial therapeutic targets to enhance the efficacy and durability of immunotherapy responses. The findings will directly inform clinical trial design for epigenetic-drug and immunotherapy combinations in MSI cancers.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR screens for immunotherapy targets exist, none have specifically and comprehensively targeted the full compendium of epigenetic writers, erasers, and readers in a patient-derived, immunocompetent organoid model that recapitulates the tumour-immune microenvironment and therapy-induced selective pressure.

proposals.title: Epigenetic Immune Sculpting in Premalignancy: Genome-wide Methylation Loss and T-cell Clonotype Dynamics in Pre-Neoplastic Lesions
proposals.motivation: Cancer immunoediting is a central paradigm, but its epigenetic initiation in pre-neoplastic stages remains opaque. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab, established that global hypomethylation is a hallmark of advanced cancers and is associated with immune evasion and relapse. However, it is unknown if this epigenetic dysregulation begins in premalignant lesions, potentially shaping the earliest interactions between incipient tumor cells and the immune system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genome-wide loss of DNA methylation (GW-LoM) is an early event in pre-neoplastic progression that creates a permissive epigenetic landscape for immune editing, characterized by the expansion of specific T-cell receptor (TCR) clonotypes and altered immune infiltration.
proposals.method: We will perform high-depth, genome-wide DNA methylation profiling (Illumina EPIC arrays and oxidative bisulfite sequencing for 5hmC) on microdissected premalignant lesions (e.g., Barrett's esophagus, colonic adenomas, lung AAH) and matched normal tissue. This will be integrated with TCR sequencing (TCR-seq) from the same lesions to assess T-cell clonality. Statistical evaluation of prognostic models will be adapted to identify methylation alterations associated with specific TCR repertoires. Multi-optic correlation will use non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) to deconvolute co-varying epigenetic and immune signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the epigenetic timeline of immune editing, identifying whether GW-LoM is a cause or consequence of early immune pressure. It will provide a novel biomarker framework for stratifying premalignant lesions based on their epigenetic-immune status, with direct implications for cancer interception strategies.
proposals.novelty: While epigenetic studies in cancer are common, applying deep methylation and immune repertoire profiling to scarce, anatomically defined premalignant lesions at single-lesion resolution is novel. Linking specific epigenetic alterations to the emergent T-cell clonotype landscape addresses a fundamental gap in understanding how immunoediting is epigenetically programmed from its inception.

proposals.title: Integrated Epigenomic and Transcriptomic Profiling Reveals Tumor-Intrinsic Epigenetic Programs that Dictate Immune Microenvironment Composition and Clinical Relapse
proposals.motivation: Current cancer biology often artificially separates tumor-intrinsic genetic/epigenetic alterations from the composition and function of the tumor immune microenvironment (TIME). This dichotomy limits our understanding of how somatic events in cancer cells directly sculpt the immune landscape, a critical determinant of therapy response and relapse. A systematic, multi-omic investigation is needed to formally challenge this separation and identify unifying mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, recurrent epigenetic alterations in cancer cells, particularly in chromatin regulatory genes and non-promoter regulatory elements, establish cell-autonomous transcriptional programs that actively recruit, exclude, or modulate specific immune cell populations, thereby creating permissive or restrictive microenvironments for relapse.
proposals.method: We will perform integrated whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS), histone modification ChIP-seq (H3K4me3, H3K27ac), and single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) on a cohort of 50 paired primary and relapsed non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) samples. Tumor-intrinsic epigenetic states will be correlated with immune cell composition deconvoluted from scRNA-seq using CIBERSORTx. We will employ multivariate Cox regression and LASSO modeling to identify epigenetic signatures predictive of TIME composition and relapse-free survival, functionally validating top candidate regulatory elements via CRISPRi/a in co-culture assays with autologous T cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, mechanistic framework linking tumor cell epigenetics to immune evasion, moving beyond descriptive correlations. It will deliver a novel epigenetic signature predictive of TIME state and relapse risk, offering potential biomarkers for immunotherapy stratification. The findings will challenge the prevailing binary classification in oncology, demonstrating that tumor-intrinsic and microenvironmental categories are functionally inseparable.
proposals.novelty: While TIME composition is well-studied, this proposal uniquely positions tumor cell epigenetics as the central, causal driver, not just a correlative feature. It leverages Dr. Esteller's expertise in cancer epigenomics to directly test causality in immune shaping, a non-obvious extension of his work on chromatin in pluripotency and cancer.

proposals.title: Functional Validation of Epigenetic and Transcriptional Drivers of Exceptional Therapeutic Response in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: Exceptional responders to cancer therapy represent a unique opportunity to identify actionable mechanisms of sensitivity and resistance. Pre-2026, integrative multi-omics analyses of such patients have generated hypotheses linking specific epigenetic alterations, chromatin states, and non-coding RNA profiles to profound treatment responses. However, these computational associations require direct experimental validation to distinguish causal drivers from passenger events and to inform the development of predictive biomarkers and rational combination therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific chromatin accessibility signatures and DNA methylation patterns, particularly in regulatory regions of genes involved in glucocorticoid receptor signaling and IκBα/NF-κB pathways, are functionally responsible for the exceptional therapeutic response observed in a subset of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients.
proposals.method: We will employ patient-derived organoids (PDOs) and CRISPR-based epigenetic editing (e.g., dCas9-DNMT3A, dCas9-TET1, dCas9-p300) in established NSCLC cell lines to recapitulate or disrupt the candidate epigenetic alterations identified in exceptional responders. Functional validation will involve assays for drug sensitivity (e.g., to chemotherapy/targeted agents), chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) for histone modifications and glucocorticoid receptor binding, and assay for transposase-accessible chromatin sequencing (ATAC-seq). Transcriptomic changes will be assessed by RNA-seq, and statistical evaluation of the association between engineered alterations and therapy response will be performed using generalized linear models.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlation to establish causal links between specific epigenetic states and extreme therapy sensitivity in NSCLC. It will generate a functionally validated shortlist of epigenetic drivers that could serve as biomarkers for patient stratification. Furthermore, it will elucidate novel biology at the intersection of chromatin regulation, inflammatory pathways, and treatment response, suggesting new therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have cataloged omics alterations in responders, this proposal uniquely focuses on the direct functional interrogation of epigenetic mechanisms derived from integrative analysis, specifically testing their role in modulating key signaling pathways (glucocorticoid, NF-κB) implicated in cancer cell survival and therapy relapse.

proposals.title: Systematic Validation of Rare Epigenetic and Genetic Lesions as Determinants of Exceptional Therapeutic Response in Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Exceptional responders to cancer therapy represent a unique opportunity to identify predictive biomarkers. However, pre-2026 studies often focus on common genetic alterations, while rare lesions in non-coding regions or involving epigenetic regulators remain under-validated. A systematic, multi-omics approach is needed to confirm their prognostic and predictive value, which could unlock new therapeutic strategies for broader patient populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Rare genetic and epigenetic lesions, particularly in chromatin remodelers and non-coding regulatory elements, are causally associated with exceptional therapeutic responses and can serve as robust biomarkers for predicting patient outcomes and relapse risk.
proposals.method: We will perform integrated whole-genome sequencing, whole-genome bisulfite sequencing, and chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq for H3K27ac, H3K4me3) on pre-treatment samples from a curated cohort of exceptional responders and matched non-responders. Lesions will be identified using Mutect2 for somatic variants, EpiDISH for cell-type deconvolution of methylation arrays, and MACS2 for peak calling. Statistical validation of association with progression-free survival will employ Cox proportional-hazards models, with correction for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated catalog of rare lesions with predictive power for therapy success, moving beyond common driver mutations. It will establish a framework for integrating rare epigenetic alterations into clinical biomarker discovery, potentially identifying new therapeutic targets derived from the biology of exceptional responders.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links common mutations to response, this proposal uniquely focuses on systematic validation of *rare* lesions, integrating epigenetic states and non-coding variants—a non-obvious source of predictive signal—using a multi-omics framework tailored to Dr. Esteller's expertise in cancer epigenomics.

proposals.title: Targeting the Epigenetic and Progenitor Cell Landscape to Overcome Resistance to VEGF Inhibition in AFP-High Hepatocellular Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP)-high hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is an aggressive subtype associated with progenitor cell features, chemoresistance, and poor prognosis. While VEGF inhibitors are a cornerstone of advanced HCC therapy, resistance and relapse are common, driven by tumor heterogeneity and adaptive survival mechanisms. Pre-2026 work indicates that progenitor-like cancer cells possess distinct epigenetic and transcriptional programs that may confer resilience to anti-angiogenic therapy, creating an urgent need for rational combinatorial strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AFP-high HCCs rely on a specific epigenetic and transcriptional program that maintains progenitor-cell features, and that co-targeting this program alongside VEGF signaling will synergistically induce tumor regression and prevent relapse by eliminating resistant cell populations.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-omics analysis of pre-treatment and post-relapse AFP-high HCC patient samples (DNA methylation arrays, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq for H3K27ac) to identify epigenetic alterations and gene expression networks associated with progenitor features and VEGF inhibitor resistance. Using established HCC cell lines and patient-derived organoids stratified by AFP status, we will test combinatorial therapies pairing VEGF receptor inhibitors (e.g., lenvatinib) with pharmacological agents targeting identified vulnerabilities (e.g., EZH2 or BET inhibitors). Efficacy and mechanistic validation will be assessed through functional assays (proliferation, apoptosis, sphere formation) and in vivo xenograft models, with statistical evaluation of synergistic drug interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the epigenetic drivers of progenitor-cell features in AFP-high HCC and establish a novel, mechanistically grounded combinatorial therapy strategy. It will provide a biomarker framework for patient stratification and directly address the critical clinical challenge of therapeutic resistance, moving beyond empirical combination regimens.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of epigenetic profiling—a core expertise of Dr. Esteller—with functional oncology to rationally design a combination therapy specifically for the aggressive AFP-high HCC subtype, rather than applying generic anti-angiogenic combinations.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Deconvolution of Cellular Heterogeneity and Progenitor Lineages in Alpha-Fetoprotein-High Hepatocellular Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) with high serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is associated with aggressive clinical behavior, therapy resistance, and poor prognosis. Pre-2026 bulk-tissue analyses have linked AFP-high status to stem-like gene signatures, but the precise cellular composition and hierarchy of malignant and stromal populations driving this phenotype remain poorly resolved. Deconvolving this heterogeneity is critical for understanding the cellular origins of relapse and identifying novel therapeutic targets for this high-risk subgroup.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AFP-high HCCs are characterized by a unique cellular ecosystem dominated by specific malignant progenitor states and a permissive tumor microenvironment that collectively drive aggressive biology and treatment failure.
proposals.method: We will perform droplet-based single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics Chromium) on fresh tumor and matched non-tumor liver tissues from 15 AFP-high (>400 ng/mL) and 15 AFP-low HCC patients. Cell type annotation will be performed using reference-based (SingleR) and clustering-based (Seurat) approaches. Trajectory inference and lineage analysis of malignant cells will be conducted using Monocle3 and RNA velocity (scVelo) to identify progenitor states. Differential expression and cell-cell communication (CellChat) analyses will compare cellular programs and interactions between AFP-high and AFP-low ecosystems.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution atlas of the cellular architecture of AFP-high HCC, defining the transcriptomic states of malignant progenitor cells and their associated microenvironment. It will identify candidate master regulators and cell communication networks that sustain the AFP-high phenotype, offering new biomarkers and potential therapeutic vulnerabilities to prevent relapse in this aggressive disease subtype.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has associated AFP with stemness signatures in bulk tissue, this proposal applies single-cell resolution specifically to deconvolve the AFP-high phenotype, directly linking cellular lineage states and ecosystem interactions to a key clinical prognostic factor, which has not been comprehensively done.

proposals.title: Deconvolution of Cell-Free DNA Methylation Signatures for the Early Detection and Tissue-of-Origin Mapping of Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, liquid biopsies using circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) have shown promise for cancer detection and monitoring, but their sensitivity for detecting minimal residual disease (MRD) and early relapse remains limited. A major challenge is that cfDNA in plasma is a composite of fragments from multiple cell types, potentially diluting tumor-specific epigenetic signals. This project addresses the critical need for more sensitive, specific biomarkers to predict relapse in cancer patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cell-type-specific DNA methylation signatures within cfDNA can be computationally deconvoluted to reveal a reliable composite biomarker that is more sensitive for detecting minimal residual disease and predicting cancer relapse than bulk cfDNA analysis.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) on serial plasma cfDNA samples from a cohort of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients in remission, alongside matched tumor and normal tissue. Using a reference-based deconvolution approach, we will apply algorithms (e.g., MethAtlas, EpiDISH) to quantify the proportional contribution of different cell types, including the cancer cell of origin, to the cfDNA methylome. Statistical models will associate alterations in these deconvoluted proportions with subsequent clinical relapse.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for interpreting cfDNA methylomes as a composite of cell-type-specific signals. It will provide a proof-of-concept for a highly sensitive epigenetic liquid biopsy to monitor MRD, directly addressing the clinical challenge of predicting relapse. The findings could translate into a new prognostic tool for personalized surveillance strategies.
proposals.novelty: While cfDNA methylation for cancer detection is established, the explicit deconvolution of its composite nature to create a relapse-specific biomarker is novel. This moves beyond simply identifying cancer-associated hyper/hypomethylation to dynamically quantifying the shifting cellular origins of cfDNA, which may be the earliest indicator of disease recurrence.

proposals.title: Inferring the Pre-Response Epigenome of Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes: A Computational Framework for Epigenetic Therapy Design in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), a significant proportion of patients experience primary resistance or eventual relapse. Pre-2026 studies, including our own prognostic model analyses, highlight the critical but poorly understood role of the epigenetic state of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) in determining therapeutic outcome. A systematic, inverse-engineering approach to define the optimal epigenetic landscape for durable response is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, conserved epigenetic state in CD8+ TILs, characterized by defined DNA methylation and histone modification profiles, is necessary for a durable anti-tumor response to ICB, and that this state can be computationally inferred from responder profiles and pharmacologically induced.
proposals.method: We will integrate single-cell ATAC-seq and RNA-seq data from pre-treatment NSCLC patient biopsies (responders vs. non-responders) to model chromatin accessibility and gene expression. Using a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture, we will learn a latent representation of the TIL epigenome. A supervised classifier will be trained on this latent space to predict response, and its gradients will be used to solve the inverse problem, identifying the latent vector representing the 'optimal responder state'. Differential analysis will pinpoint specific regulatory elements and genes (e.g., exhaustion markers, memory precursors) whose epigenetic alterations are associated with this state.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first computational map of the 'ideal' pre-response epigenetic configuration of TILs in NSCLC. It will directly nominate specific epigenetic targets (e.g., promoters, enhancers) for pharmacological intervention. The framework provides a novel strategy to move beyond correlative biomarkers to a causal, engineering-based approach for designing combination epigenetic-immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: While existing studies correlate epigenetic alterations with outcome, this project uniquely frames it as an inverse optimization problem to computationally derive a target state for engineering, bridging deep learning-based representation learning with actionable therapeutic design in immuno-oncology.

proposals.title: A Composite Multi-Omic Network Model Integrating DNA Methylation for Robust Prediction of Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: Current prognostic models in oncology, particularly for relapse risk, often rely on single-omics data (e.g., methylation signatures) or simple additive multi-omic scores, which fail to capture the complex compensatory interactions between molecular layers. This limits their robustness and accuracy in heterogeneous patient populations. A framework that explicitly models these biological interactions is needed to create more reliable clinical prediction tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a structured interaction network between DNA methylation alterations and other molecular factors (e.g., transcriptomic, proteomic) will generate a composite risk score that is more robust and accurate for predicting cancer relapse than models based on any single data layer or their linear combination.
proposals.method: We will build a multi-layer network using patient-matched DNA methylation (Infinium EPIC array), RNA-seq, and targeted proteomic/metabolomic data from NSCLC cohorts with relapse annotation. Network edges will represent statistically significant interactions (e.g., methylation-transcription correlations conditioned on outcome) inferred using Gaussian Graphical Models and context-specific Bayesian networks. The final composite risk score will be derived from the network's stability under simulated 'stress' (e.g., bootstrapping, noise injection) using graph-based resilience metrics, analogous to material failure models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for multi-omic integration that moves beyond additive models. It will provide a biologically interpretable network model of molecular interactions driving relapse and a validated, robust composite risk score for clinical translation in NSCLC, with potential applicability to other cancers.
