proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the engineering principle of composite material failure to model biological resilience, explicitly capturing compensatory interactions between omics layers rather than treating them as independent contributors. This network-based 'stress-test' approach to risk score generation is distinct from existing ensemble or linear models.

proposals.title: Single-Cell DNA Methylation Deconvolution of Peripheral Blood Identifies Cell-Type-Specific Epigenetic Drivers of Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease
proposals.motivation: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) remains a leading cause of mortality, yet its molecular pathogenesis is incompletely understood. Bulk DNA methylation studies of blood have identified associations with CVD risk, but these signals are confounded by cellular heterogeneity, masking the specific contributions of individual immune and vascular cell populations. Deconvolving these signals is critical to identify the precise cell types and epigenetic alterations driving disease progression and relapse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-cell DNA methylation profiling of peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from CVD patients will reveal cell-type-specific and cell-state-specific epigenetic alterations associated with atherosclerotic burden and clinical outcomes, which are obscured in bulk analyses.
proposals.method: We will apply high-throughput single-cell bisulfite sequencing (scBS-seq) to PBMCs from patients with severe coronary artery disease and matched controls. Cell types will be annotated using integrated single-cell methylation and transcriptome (scRNA-seq) references. Differential methylation analysis will be performed per cell type using algorithms like methylSig or DSS, focusing on regions associated with CVD in prior EWAS. We will validate alterations in sorted cell populations and correlate specific epigenetic signatures with imaging-based plaque burden and future cardiovascular events.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution map of cell-type-specific epigenetic dysregulation in human CVD. It will pinpoint the specific immune subsets (e.g., monocyte subsets, T cell states) most epigenetically perturbed in disease, providing mechanistic insights into inflammation and plaque instability. The identified signatures could serve as novel biomarkers for disease stratification and relapse risk.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Esteller's lab has pioneered cancer epigenomics and bulk tissue analysis, applying single-cell methylation deconvolution specifically to CVD is novel. This approach moves beyond associative bulk signals to identify causal cell-type-specific drivers, a strategy not yet widely applied in complex cardiovascular epidemiology.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic Machine Learning Framework Integrating DNA Methylation and Transcriptomics for the Prediction of Relapse in Early-Stage Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite curative surgery, a significant fraction of early-stage colorectal cancer (CRC) patients experience relapse, indicating a need for improved risk stratification beyond conventional clinicopathological factors. Pre-2026, DNA methylation alterations, a hallmark of cancer studied extensively by Dr. Esteller, and transcriptomic dysregulation are known to be associated with tumor aggressiveness, but their integrated prognostic power remains underexploited. Current models often rely on single data types, failing to capture the complex, multi-layered biology driving recurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model integrating genome-wide DNA methylation data with gene expression profiles will identify a superior, biologically interpretable multi-omic signature that more accurately stratifies early-stage CRC patients by risk of relapse than models based on either data type alone.
proposals.method: We will utilize pre-treatment tumor samples from a well-annotated cohort of stage I-III CRC patients with known relapse status. DNA methylation (Illumina EPIC array) and RNA-seq data will be processed and normalized using established pre-2026 pipelines. Feature selection will be performed using penalized regression (LASSO) on each omics layer separately, followed by integration. We will train and compare several ML architectures, including a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) and a Random Forest classifier, to model the non-linear interactions between selected methylation and expression features. Model performance will be rigorously validated via nested cross-validation and on an independent external cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, clinically applicable multi-omic biomarker signature for relapse risk in early-stage CRC. It will provide a computational framework for integrating methylation and transcriptomic data, a resource for the community. The identified features will offer novel biological insights into the epigenetic and transcriptional programs underlying disease progression and therapeutic failure.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to single omics data in cancer, the systematic integration of methylation—a stable and early epigenetic mark—with dynamic transcriptomic states using non-linear models for relapse prediction in early-stage CRC is novel. This approach moves beyond simple correlation studies to build a predictive, unified model of disease outcome.

proposals.title: Colocalization Analysis of DNA Methylation Quantitative Trait Loci and Disease-Associated Variants to Identify Shared Causal Genetic Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of genetic variants associated with complex diseases and traits, including cancer. Concurrently, methylation quantitative trait locus (meQTL) studies have mapped genetic variants influencing DNA methylation. However, it remains unclear whether the same underlying causal genetic variant drives both the epigenetic alteration and the disease risk, which is crucial for understanding mechanistic pathways and identifying therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of disease-associated genetic loci identified in GWAS colocalize with meQTLs, indicating shared causal variants that influence disease risk through epigenetic regulation.
proposals.method: We will apply established genetic colocalization methods, specifically the Bayesian statistical framework implemented in the 'coloc' R package (pre-2026 standard), to large-scale, pre-existing datasets. We will integrate summary statistics from public cancer GWAS (e.g., for non-small cell lung cancer relapse) with meQTL data derived from matched normal and tumor tissue from repositories like TCGA. The analysis will systematically test for shared causal variants (posterior probability > 0.8) at each locus, followed by functional annotation using chromatin activity and gene expression data to prioritize mechanistic links.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic map of disease loci where genetic risk is likely mediated by DNA methylation changes, distinguishing causal epigenetic drivers from passenger events. It will generate novel, testable hypotheses about the regulatory pathways linking genetic variation to cancer initiation, progression, or relapse, directly informing the development of epigenetic biomarkers and therapies.
proposals.novelty: While colocalization is standard in genetics (e.g., for GWAS and eQTLs), its rigorous application to integrate epigenetic QTLs (meQTLs) with disease associations in cancer is not yet routine. This project formally adapts this powerful causal inference framework to the core epigenetic research of Dr. Esteller, moving beyond correlation to test for shared genetic causality.

proposals.title: Mapping the Epigenetic Energy Landscape: A Hysteresis Framework for Irreversible DNA Methylation and Chromatin States in Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: Despite the development of epigenetic therapies, many cancers relapse after treatment, suggesting a subset of epigenetic alterations are not reversed. Current models attribute this to sustained oncogenic signaling, but they fail to explain the persistence of these alterations even after signal removal, a major clinical hurdle in achieving durable remissions. This project aims to investigate an alternative, thermodynamic explanation for this irreversibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific cancer-associated epigenetic states, particularly in DNA methylation and histone modifications, exhibit hysteresis; they are driven into deep, alternative energy minima in the chromatin landscape and become 'locked-in' by molecular pinning sites, creating an energy barrier that prevents therapeutic reversion to a normal epigenetic state.
proposals.method: We will employ single-cell multi-omics (scATAC-seq, scRNA-seq, and scBS-seq) on paired pre-treatment and post-relapse samples from non-small cell lung cancer patients to track epigenetic state transitions. Using these data, we will construct a chromatin state energy landscape via computational methods like Potential of Mean Force (PMF) calculations and Markov State Models. Candidate molecular 'pinning sites' (e.g., persistent chromatin remodeler complexes, DNA lesions, or nuclear lamina interactions) will be identified through integrated proteomics (ChIP-MS) and Hi-C analyses in isogenic cell line models before and after epigenetic drug exposure and withdrawal.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel thermodynamic framework for understanding epigenetic irreversibility in cancer, moving beyond linear signaling models. It will provide a predictive model to identify which epigenetic alterations in a patient's tumor are likely to be resistant to reversal, informing combination therapy strategies and prognostic models for relapse risk.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the physical concept of hysteresis and energy landscape theory to cancer epigenetics, a departure from the dominant gene-centric and signaling-pathway perspectives. It directly investigates the system-level properties of chromatin that confer memory and resistance, rather than focusing solely on the enzymes that write epigenetic marks.

proposals.title: Quantifying Collateral Cytotoxicity: A Systematic Evaluation of Non-Specific Antiproliferative Effects in Epigenetic Cancer Therapies
proposals.motivation: Epigenetic drugs like DNMT and HDAC inhibitors are clinically approved but show variable efficacy and toxicity, often attributed to heterogeneous target expression or off-target effects. Pre-2026, the field predominantly focuses on their specific, target-mediated mechanisms, potentially overlooking a significant component of their therapeutic action. This gap is critical because understanding the full basis of efficacy is essential for predicting patient response, managing relapse, and rationally designing combination therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a substantial part of the therapeutic effect of approved epigenetic drugs stems from non-specific cytotoxic stress on highly proliferative cells, independent of their canonical epigenetic targets, analogous to the 'dirty drug' model in antimicrobial therapy.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR-Cas9 to generate isogenic cancer cell line pairs (e.g., HCT-116, A549) with knockout of canonical targets (e.g., DNMT1, HDAC1/2). These and parental cells will be treated with approved drugs (azacitidine, decitabine, vorinostat, romidepsin). We will systematically measure cell death (Annexin V/PI), proliferation (EdU incorporation), and general stress markers (ROS, γH2AX). Statistical evaluation will compare dose-response curves and stress induction between target-proficient and -deficient cells across proliferative vs. quiescent states to quantify the 'collateral cytotoxicity' contribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, quantitative framework for dissecting target-specific versus non-specific effects of epigenetic therapies. It will offer a mechanistic explanation for differential responses in heterogeneous tumors and relapse, potentially informing more effective dosing schedules and rational combinations with other cytotoxic agents.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the dominant paradigm of epigenetic drug action by directly testing an analogy from infectious disease, challenging the primary assumption of mechanism-of-action. It applies a rigorous, target-disabled genetic approach to a question typically addressed only pharmacologically.

proposals.title: Dynamic Chromatin Foreshocks Outperform Static DNA Methylation in Predicting Early Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: DNA methylation is a cornerstone of cancer epigenetics, providing stable diagnostic and prognostic signatures. However, its static nature may fail to capture the dynamic, rapid molecular shifts preceding critical clinical events like relapse. In seismology, prioritizing dynamic precursor events over static strain measurements improved prediction; a similar paradigm shift is needed in oncology to achieve earlier, more actionable detection of disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic chromatin state alterations, measured by specific histone modification turnovers and transient accessibility changes, are superior to static DNA methylation patterns in predicting imminent cancer relapse, offering a more sensitive and timely diagnostic signal.
proposals.method: We will perform longitudinal, multi-omics profiling (ChIP-seq for H3K27ac, H3K4me3; ATAC-seq; and methylation arrays) on serial patient samples (primary tumor, post-treatment, relapse) from non-small cell lung cancer cohorts with annotated relapse data. We will develop a prognostic prediction model using a machine learning architecture (e.g., a time-aware recurrent neural network or a Cox proportional hazards model with regularization) that explicitly weights the rate-of-change (delta) in chromatin features more heavily than static methylation values. Model performance for predicting time-to-relapse will be statistically evaluated against models based solely on methylation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for cancer prognostication that prioritizes dynamic epigenetic information, potentially identifying new, actionable biomarkers for relapse risk. It will provide a proof-of-concept that chromatin 'foreshocks' can refine clinical prediction models, moving the field toward more responsive monitoring strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, systematic comparison and prioritization of dynamic chromatin signals over the well-established static methylation landscape for clinical prediction, a concept inspired by seismic forecasting that has not been rigorously applied in cancer epigenetics.

proposals.title: Epigenetic Determinants of Therapeutic Response and Acquired Resistance in Solid Tumors: A Multi-Omic Biomarker Discovery Study
proposals.motivation: Epigenetic therapies, such as DNA methyltransferase and histone deacetylase inhibitors, show variable and often transient efficacy in cancer. A major clinical challenge is the inability to predict which patients will respond and the inevitable emergence of resistance, leading to relapse. Identifying robust epigenetic biomarkers for drug choice and resistance is critical for personalizing therapy and improving long-term outcomes, particularly in cancers with limited treatment options.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, pre-therapy DNA methylation and chromatin accessibility patterns in tumor cells and the tumor microenvironment are predictive of initial response to epigenetic and standard chemotherapies. Furthermore, we posit that the acquisition of resistance is driven by dynamic, therapy-induced epigenetic reprogramming that can be detected in cell-free DNA.
proposals.method: We will perform a multi-omic analysis on longitudinal patient samples (pre-treatment, on-treatment, and relapse) from clinical trials of epigenetic drugs (e.g., azacitidine) combined with standard therapies. Using reduced-representation bisulfite sequencing (RRBS) for DNA methylation and ATAC-seq for chromatin accessibility, we will profile tumor biopsies and matched plasma-derived cell-free DNA. We will employ statistical models (e.g., LASSO regression) and machine learning algorithms (random forests, gradient boosting) to identify predictive epigenetic signatures of response. To model the emergence of resistance, we will analyze temporal alterations using differential methylation/accessibility analysis and correlate these with gene expression from available RNA-seq data.
proposals.contribution: This study will deliver a validated panel of epigenetic biomarkers for predicting therapeutic response and monitoring resistance emergence in real-time via liquid biopsy. It will provide mechanistic insights into how epigenetic landscapes evolve under therapeutic pressure, linking specific alterations to relapse. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective, epigenetically-guided combination therapies and adaptive treatment strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have linked static epigenetic marks to prognosis, this proposal uniquely focuses on dynamic, therapy-induced epigenetic changes as drivers of acquired resistance, integrating multi-omic tumor and liquid biopsy data longitudinally. The application of these methods to predict and track resistance to epigenetic therapies themselves, rather than just standard chemotherapy, represents a critical and underexplored frontier.

proposals.title: Combinatorial PROTAC-mediated degradation of BET and YEATS domain proteins reveals non-redundant roles and adaptive epigenetic networks in non-small cell lung cancer
proposals.motivation: Epigenetic reader proteins, such as those containing BET or YEATS domains, are promising therapeutic targets in cancer, but functional redundancy within families can lead to treatment resistance and relapse. Pre-2026 studies using single inhibitors often fail to reveal the full network dependencies and compensatory mechanisms, limiting their clinical efficacy. A systematic, combinatorial approach is needed to dissect these complex interactions and identify optimal therapeutic nodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute, combinatorial degradation of multiple epigenetic reader proteins (e.g., BRD2/3/4 and ENL/AF9) will unmask their unique, non-redundant functions and reveal critical, targetable vulnerabilities in the epigenetic network of NSCLC cells that are masked by single-agent approaches.
proposals.method: We will employ PROTACs targeting BET (BRD2/3/4) and YEATS domain (ENL, AF9) proteins in NSCLC cell lines, using single, double, and triple degrader combinations. Acute degradation effects will be captured by simultaneous RNA-seq (for transcriptomic changes) and mass spectrometry-based proteomics (for chromatin complex remodeling) at 0, 6, and 24-hour timepoints. Data integration and network analysis will be performed using established pre-2026 tools (e.g., weighted gene co-expression network analysis - WGCNA) to map immediate direct effects and subsequent adaptive responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a high-resolution map of functional interdependencies within the epigenetic reader network in cancer. It will identify specific combinatorial degradation strategies that are synthetically lethal, providing a rationale for novel combination therapies to prevent compensatory adaptation and relapse. The integrated multi-omic profiling dataset will serve as a public resource for the epigenetics community.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond single-target inhibition or genetic knockout, this study uses acute, combinatorial pharmacological degradation to dynamically probe epigenetic network plasticity and redundancy in a clinically relevant timeframe, a strategy not comprehensively applied to reader protein families in cancer models pre-2026.

proposals.title: A CRISPR-dCas9-based biosensor-effector platform for context-aware epigenetic stabilization against cancer relapse-associated alterations
proposals.motivation: Epigenetic instability is a key driver of cancer progression and relapse, yet therapeutic strategies to lock cells into a fixed epigenetic state risk impairing essential physiological plasticity. There is a critical unmet need for systems that can protect epigenetic integrity in disease-relevant contexts, such as against the dysregulated chromatin activity seen in cancer, while permitting adaptive remodeling for normal cellular functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop epigenetic control system, using biosensors of key cellular states to dynamically regulate engineered epigenetic modifiers, can create a homeostatic epigenetic basin that resists disease-driving perturbations without abolishing necessary plasticity.
proposals.method: We will engineer a modular platform where a dCas9-based epigenetic writer (e.g., DNMT3A or EZH2 fusion) is placed under the control of synthetic promoters responsive to a biosensor suite. Biosensors will monitor context via: 1) a hypoxia-response element (HRE) promoter, 2) a NF-κB response element for inflammatory stress, and 3) a synthetic promoter reporting on the expression of a core pluripotency factor (e.g., OCT4). The system's logic will be implemented using CRISPRi to repress the effector in permissive contexts (e.g., hypoxia, differentiation signals) and activate it in target contexts (e.g., normoxic cancer cells). Validation will be performed in patient-derived cancer cell lines and models of therapy-induced plasticity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-in-class prototype for dynamic epigenetic guardianship, moving beyond static editing. It will provide a novel framework to understand how to engineer epigenetic resilience, with direct implications for preventing cancer relapse driven by epigenetic alterations. The platform could be adapted to other diseases of epigenetic dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, context-aware control of epigenetic modifiers, which is a significant departure from existing constitutive or drug-inducible epigenetic editing tools, aiming for autonomous maintenance of a healthy epigenetic basin.

proposals.title: Deep Learning-Based Identification of Protective Epigenetic Signatures for the Design of Epigenetic Stabilizers in Cancer Therapy
proposals.motivation: Current epigenetic therapies, like DNA methyltransferase inhibitors, aim to reverse aberrant marks associated with disease, but they often lack specificity and can induce widespread, unintended epigenetic alterations. A more precise therapeutic strategy would be to actively reinforce naturally occurring, beneficial epigenetic configurations that are associated with healthy cellular states, therapeutic response, or delayed relapse. This is particularly relevant in oncology, where epigenetic instability contributes to treatment resistance and disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep learning models can be trained on high-dimensional epigenetic profiles from patient cohorts to identify specific, stable multi-modal epigenetic signatures (integrating DNA methylation, histone modifications, and chromatin accessibility) that are strongly predictive of positive clinical outcomes, such as durable remission or longevity, and that these signatures represent viable targets for pharmacological stabilization.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-2026 deep learning architectures, specifically hybrid convolutional neural network-transformer (CNN-Transformer) models, adapted for multi-omics integration. These models will be trained on publicly available datasets (e.g., from TCGA, ICGC) and our own patient cohorts, using DNA methylation arrays, ChIP-seq, and ATAC-seq data as input. The primary output will be a prediction of relapse-free survival. Using integrated gradient and attention mechanisms, the model will pinpoint the most contributory genomic loci and epigenetic marks defining the 'optimal' protective signature. Candidate stabilizer compounds will then be screened in vitro for their ability to reinforce this signature using engineered reporter cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for defining positive epigenetic states, moving beyond disease-associated alterations. It will generate a prioritized list of genomic loci and epigenetic configurations that constitute a 'protective epigenome' signature for specific cancer types. Furthermore, it will provide a proof-of-concept for a new drug discovery pipeline targeting epigenetic maintenance, directly aligned with Dr. Esteller's expertise in linking epigenetic alterations to cancer relapse.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning has been used to predict epigenetic states or disease outcomes from genomic data, its application to explicitly define and then pharmacologically target *beneficial* epigenetic configurations for stabilization represents a significant paradigm shift from corrective to preservative epigenetics.

proposals.title: Spatial Epigenomic Mapping of the Liver Niche: Integrating Spatial Transcriptomics with Inferred DNA Methylation to Decipher Architectural Drivers of Hepatocellular Carcinoma Relapse
proposals.motivation: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) relapse is strongly associated with alterations in the tumor microenvironment and epigenetic dysregulation, particularly DNA methylation. However, pre-2026 methods lack the spatial resolution to link specific methylation patterns within the complex architecture of the liver—including zonation, immune niches, and fibrotic regions—to local transcriptional programs and clinical outcomes. This gap limits our understanding of how spatial epigenetic heterogeneity drives recurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially resolved, inferred DNA methylation patterns within the liver's architectural niches will reveal distinct epigenetic- transcriptional modules that are associated with high-risk microenvironments and predictive of post-surgical HCC relapse.
proposals.method: We will apply 10x Genomics Visium spatial transcriptomics to FFPE tissue sections from a cohort of HCC patients with documented relapse or long-term remission. Using pre-2026 computational frameworks (e.g., SPOTlight for deconvolution and PRISM for cell-type annotation), we will define architectural niches. We will then infer spatial DNA methylation patterns by applying a validated algorithm, such as MethylSPW or a transfer learning model trained on paired bulk/single-cell RNA-seq and methylation data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), to the spatially resolved gene expression data. Statistical evaluation of prognostic models will integrate these spatial epigenetic features with clinical variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially resolved epigenomic atlas of the liver in HCC, identifying novel methylation signatures tied to specific microenvironments. It will establish a framework for spatial methylation inference applicable to other cancers and contribute a statistically evaluated prognostic model that incorporates spatial epigenetic features to predict relapse risk more accurately.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Esteller's prior work has extensively catalogued methylation alterations in cancer, this proposal uniquely integrates spatial transcriptomics with methylation inference to move beyond bulk-tissue or single-cell suspensions, preserving the critical architectural context that governs cellular function and disease progression.

proposals.title: Systematic Epigenomic and Transcriptomic Profiling of Recurrently Mutated Proteins (RMPs) to Define a Cancer-Specific Therapeutic Vulnerability Window
proposals.motivation: Targeted cancer therapies often fail due to on-target, off-tissue toxicity, where inhibiting a mutated protein disrupts its essential function in healthy cells. Pre-2026, the field lacks a comprehensive framework to systematically distinguish which recurrently mutated proteins (RMPs) in cancer have functions so uniquely rewired in the malignant state that their inhibition would spare normal tissues. This gap hinders the development of truly specific, low-toxicity treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of RMPs exhibits a 'cancer-specific dependency window,' where their essential protein-protein interactions or chromatin-regulatory functions are fundamentally altered in tumors compared to their normal tissue-of-origin counterparts, making them uniquely vulnerable to targeted inhibition with minimal off-tissue effects.
proposals.method: We will integrate multi-omics data from TCGA and GTEx (pre-2026) for selected RMPs. Using chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) and assay for transposase-accessible chromatin (ATAC-seq) in matched patient-derived cancer cells and normal primary cells, we will map cancer-specific epigenetic landscapes. Protein-protein interaction networks will be constructed via affinity purification-mass spectrometry (AP-MS). Statistical evaluation and machine learning (employing random forest and gradient boosting models) will identify RMPs whose functional interactome and chromatin activity are most divergent in cancer, predicting high specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a ranked, actionable list of RMP targets predicted to have maximal cancer cell specificity, directly informing drug development priorities. It will establish a novel integrative omics framework for evaluating therapeutic specificity beyond genetic mutation status, moving the field toward a more predictive understanding of on-target toxicity.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies link mutations to prognosis, our approach uniquely focuses on comparative *functional* and *epigenomic* rewiring—not just mutation presence—to explicitly model and predict off-tissue effects, a critical but under-explored dimension in target validation.

proposals.title: Genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 screening identifies RNA modification proteins as essential vulnerabilities and biomarkers of relapse in non-small cell lung cancer
proposals.motivation: The epitranscriptome, comprising chemical modifications to RNA, is a critical layer of gene regulation dysregulated in cancer. However, a systematic, functional understanding of which RNA modification proteins (RMPs) are essential for cancer cell survival and contribute to patient relapse is lacking. Identifying these dependencies could reveal novel therapeutic targets and prognostic markers, particularly for aggressive cancers like non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) where relapse remains a major clinical challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of RMPs, including writers, erasers, and readers, are essential for NSCLC cell survival and that their genetic or epigenetic alterations are associated with poor prognosis and relapse in patients.
proposals.method: We will perform genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screens using the Brunello sgRNA library in a panel of NSCLC cell lines with diverse genetic backgrounds. Essential RMP hits will be validated through secondary screens and in vitro competition assays. We will integrate these functional data with multi-omics profiles from NSCLC patient cohorts (e.g., TCGA), analyzing associations between RMP gene expression, promoter DNA methylation (using Illumina EPIC arrays), copy number alterations, and clinical outcomes such as relapse-free survival using Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first functional map of RMP dependencies in NSCLC, directly linking essential genes to patient outcomes. It will nominate novel RMP targets for drug development and identify potential epigenetic or genetic biomarkers predictive of relapse, advancing the field of epitranscriptomics towards clinical translation.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR screens have been used to find essential genes, their focused application to systematically interrogate the entire RMP landscape in the context of clinical relapse data is novel. This approach moves beyond correlation to establish a direct functional link between RMPs, cancer cell fitness, and patient prognosis.

proposals.title: Mapping the Spatial Coordination of RNA Modification Patterns and Chromatin Architecture in Cancer
proposals.motivation: RNA modifications (RMPs) are crucial regulators of gene expression and are frequently altered in cancer, contributing to relapse and poor prognosis. However, current methods analyze RMPs and chromatin 3D structure (e.g., via Hi-C) independently, missing potential functional crosstalk. A method to simultaneously map modified transcripts and their spatial genomic context is needed to understand how epigenetic and epitranscriptomic layers coordinate oncogenic programs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific RNA modification patterns (e.g., m6A, m5C) are spatially coordinated with distinct chromatin architectural features, such as topologically associating domains (TADs) and enhancer-promoter loops, and that this coordination is dysregulated in cancer cells, driving aberrant gene expression associated with disease progression.
proposals.method: We will adapt in situ Hi-C protocols by integrating RNA modification-specific immunoprecipitation. Chromatin is crosslinked and digested, then ligated in situ. A biotinylated antibody against a specific RNA modification (e.g., anti-m6A) will be used to pull down chromatin complexes containing modified RNA. After reverse crosslinking, DNA will be sequenced using paired-end Illumina technology. Bioinformatics analysis will use the HiC-Pro pipeline for contact map generation, followed by custom algorithms to identify genomic loci statistically enriched for both chromatin contacts and specific RMP signals, correlating these with gene expression and known cancer-associated alterations.
proposals.contribution: This will generate the first genome-wide maps linking RNA modification landscapes to 3D chromatin architecture in cancer cells. It will identify novel, spatially coordinated epigenetic-epitranscriptomic hubs dysregulated in malignancy, providing mechanistic insight into relapse-associated transcriptional programs. The developed 'Hi-C-RMP' technique will be a new resource for the field.
proposals.novelty: While Hi-C maps chromatin contacts and separate methods profile RNA modifications, no pre-2026 technique directly captures the spatial coordination between a specific RMP and the 3D genome in a single assay. This fusion of chromatin conformation capture with epitranscriptomic profiling is a novel methodological advance.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Multi-Omic Profiling of Replication-Mediated Epigenetic Plasticity in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Relapse
proposals.motivation: Cancer relapse is driven by epigenetic and transcriptional heterogeneity, but the link between specific chromatin states and gene expression programs in individual cells remains poorly mapped. Replication-Mediated Plasticity (RMP), the epigenetic variability arising during DNA replication, is a key source of this heterogeneity, yet its single-cell dynamics in patient tumors are unknown. This gap limits our understanding of how epigenetic alterations contribute to therapy resistance and disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-cell multi-omics will reveal distinct RMP-associated chromatin accessibility and DNA methylation states that are directly linked to pro-relapse transcriptional programs in individual non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cells.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell multi-omics (scATAC-seq and scRNA-seq) on longitudinal patient-derived NSCLC samples (pre-treatment and post-relapse) using the 10x Genomics Multiome platform. Data integration and cell clustering will be performed using Seurat and Signac pipelines. We will computationally infer replication timing states and identify RMP-associated differentially accessible regions (DARs) and methylation loci using established algorithms (e.g., chromVAR, Cicero). These epigenetic features will be correlated with gene expression modules associated with relapse, such as stemness or drug efflux pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first single-cell atlas linking RMP-driven epigenetic heterogeneity to transcriptional states in NSCLC relapse. It will identify novel, cell-type-specific epigenetic drivers of therapeutic failure. The findings will provide a framework for developing epigenetic biomarkers to predict relapse risk and inform combination therapies targeting replication-linked plasticity.
proposals.novelty: While bulk analyses have associated RMP with cancer, this study uniquely applies single-cell multi-omics to deconvolute its role at the resolution of individual tumor and microenvironment cells during the critical transition to relapse, moving beyond correlation to direct causal inference within cellular trajectories.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Epitranscriptomic Profiling of Circulating Tumor Cells to Predict Metastatic Relapse in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: Liquid biopsies via circulating tumor cells (CTCs) offer a non-invasive window into cancer dynamics, but current DNA-based analyses miss crucial regulatory layers. RNA modifications, particularly N6-methyladenosine (m6A), are key epigenetic regulators of gene expression associated with cancer progression and therapy resistance. A pre-2026 gap exists in understanding the epitranscriptomic landscape of single CTCs and its link to clinical outcomes like relapse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific RNA modification signatures in single CTCs, distinct from bulk tumor profiles, are associated with and can predict metastatic relapse in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients.
proposals.method: We will isolate single CTCs from NSCLC patient blood samples using the CellSearch system and microfluidic enrichment. Epitranscriptomic analysis will be performed on individual CTCs using a scaled-down version of MeRIP-seq (m6A-specific methylated RNA immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing), adapted for ultra-low input via SMART-seq2 amplification. Bioinformatic analysis will employ established algorithms (e.g., exomePeak, MeTPeak) for m6A peak calling and differential analysis, correlating modification patterns with clinical relapse data from patient follow-up.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first single-cell atlas of RNA modifications in CTCs, linking specific epitranscriptomic alterations to metastatic potential. It will provide a novel, minimally invasive biomarker for predicting relapse in NSCLC, moving liquid biopsy beyond genomic alterations to dynamic regulatory states.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Esteller's prior work has extensively mapped DNA methylation in cancer, applying epitranscriptomic analysis to the rare, single-cell context of CTCs is a novel frontier. This approach combines cutting-edge single-cell omics with the clinical utility of liquid biopsy to decipher a previously inaccessible layer of cancer regulation.

proposals.title: Population-scale epitranscriptome profiling identifies RNA modification variants as novel heritable risk factors and prognostic biomarkers for colorectal cancer
proposals.motivation: The epitranscriptome, particularly N6-methyladenosine (m6A), regulates RNA stability, splicing, and translation, with established roles in cancer cell biology. However, pre-2026 studies are largely confined to cell lines or small patient cohorts, limiting the ability to link inter-individual variation in RNA modification patterns (RMPs) to cancer susceptibility and clinical outcomes. Scaling this analysis to population-level cohorts is critical to determine if RMPs constitute a new layer of cancer risk heritability beyond genetic and epigenetic alterations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, heritable patterns of RNA modifications (RMP variants) in normal tissues are associated with increased risk of developing colorectal cancer and can predict tumor aggressiveness and relapse.
proposals.method: We will perform epitranscriptomic profiling using established MeRIP-seq (m6A) and miCLIP-seq (for precise mapping) on normal colon mucosa and matched tumor samples from >1000 patients in existing biobanks (e.g., TCGA, EPICOLON). We will develop a computational pipeline to call RMP variants (quantitative trait loci for RNA modifications) by integrating modification signals with germline SNP data. Association with cancer risk will be tested using case-control logistic regression, while prognostic value for relapse will be assessed via Cox proportional-hazards models, adjusting for known genetic, epigenetic (e.g., DNA methylation), and clinical covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first population atlas of epitranscriptomic variation in cancer, identifying specific RMP variants as novel risk biomarkers. It will determine the independent contribution of the epitranscriptome to cancer heritability and provide a framework for integrating RNA modification status into multi-omics prognostic models for patient stratification.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links epitranscriptomic *alterations* in tumors to cancer progression, this study is novel in treating RMPs as heritable, pre-morbid risk variants at a population scale, directly connecting epitranscriptomic variation in normal tissue to cancer etiology and prognosis.

ideas.title: Investigating Epigenetic Hysteresis: A Thermodynamic Framework for Irreversible Cancer-Associated Alterations
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that all cancer-associated epigenetic changes are reversible by drawing an analogy from materials science and condensed matter physics. The analogous problem is the phenomenon of hysteresis, where a system's state (e.g., magnetization) becomes path-dependent and does not return to its original state after the removal of the initial driving force, due to energy barriers and pinning effects. The analogous solution involves characterizing the system's energy landscape to identify metastable states and the conditions for irreversible transitions. Adapted to cancer epigenetics, this suggests investigating whether some epigenetic alterations become fixed or 'locked-in' not merely through sustained signaling, but because they are driven into a deep, alternative energy well in the chromatin state landscape. The research would involve using tools from systems biology and single-cell epigenomics to map these putative hysteretic epigenetic states, identifying the molecular equivalents of 'pinning sites'—such as specific, persistent chromatin remodeler complexes, DNA lesions, or nuclear architectural features—that create an energy barrier preventing drug-induced reversal. The goal is to establish a thermodynamic framework for epigenetic irreversibility in cancer, moving beyond a simple linear model of drug response.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Dirty Drug' Model from Antibiotics to Epigenetic Therapies
ideas.description: This idea inverts the view of epigenetic drugs as solely targeted by exploring their potential non-specific cytotoxic effects on highly proliferative cells. The analogy is drawn from antimicrobial chemotherapy, where the efficacy of some antibiotics was re-evaluated and found to depend significantly on non-specific, broad-spectrum cytotoxic stress affecting metabolically active pathogens, not just precise target inhibition—a concept sometimes called the 'dirty drug' or collateral sensitivity model. Adapting this solution, the research would systematically test approved epigenetic drugs in experimental models where their canonical epigenetic targets (e.g., specific HDACs or DNMTs) are genetically or pharmacologically disabled. By measuring outcomes like cell death, proliferation arrest, and induction of general cellular stress (e.g., reactive oxygen species, replication stress) in highly proliferative versus quiescent cell populations, the study aims to quantify the contribution of this 'collateral cytotoxicity' to the drugs' overall therapeutic efficacy. This approach challenges the primary assumption of mechanism-of-action and could explain differential responses in heterogeneous tumors, inform dosing schedules, and guide combination therapies.

ideas.description: This research direction applies advanced deep learning models, adapted from genomics, to analyze high-dimensional epigenetic data (e.g., histone modification patterns, DNA methylation landscapes) to predict cellular fate and identify 'optimal' epigenetic configurations associated with health, longevity, or therapeutic response. The key innovation is using these models to pinpoint precise, stable epigenetic signatures that are most predictive of positive outcomes, rather than just disease states. These signatures then become the explicit molecular targets for a new class of drugs—epigenetic stabilizers—designed to reinforce and lock in these beneficial configurations, shifting the therapeutic paradigm from correction to maintenance.

Instead of permanently stabilizing a state, the system uses engineered epigenetic modifiers whose activity is dynamically regulated by a biosensor suite monitoring the cell's physiological context. The goal is to create a 'homeostatic epigenetic basin' that resists noise and disease-driving perturbations but allows controlled remodeling in response to legitimate signals for differentiation, immune activation, or stress response. This ensures cellular function remains robust and adaptive, avoiding the iatrogenic risks of epigenetic lock-in.

ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that DNA methylation is the primary diagnostic biomarker by drawing an analogy from seismology. In earthquake prediction, the field successfully shifted focus from static cumulative strain measurements to dynamic precursor events for more accurate and timely forecasts. Adapting this solution, the research would prioritize rapidly changing histone modifications or chromatin accessibility events over the more static DNA methylation signal. The adapted idea is to treat stable DNA methylation as a background state and develop diagnostic models that weight dynamic chromatin 'foreshocks'—such as specific histone modification turnovers or transient accessibility changes—more heavily. This approach aims to capture more immediate and specific biological shifts, potentially leading to earlier and more actionable disease detection.

ideas.title: Integrate Methylation Scores with Multi-Omic Networks for Robust Risk Prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the composite materials approach from structural engineering to improve disease risk prediction. In composites, different materials are combined in a structured matrix to create a system where weaknesses in one component are compensated by others, with the overall behavior modeled through their interactions. The adapted research idea is to integrate DNA methylation biomarkers with other molecular factors (e.g., proteomic, metabolomic) not by simple additive scoring, but by constructing an interaction network model. This computational framework would explicitly model the synergistic and compensatory relationships between the different omics layers, generating a composite risk score that is more robust and accurate by accounting for the biological 'stress-strain' dynamics between molecular systems, thereby creating a predictive tool stronger than any single biomarker source.

ideas.description: This research direction applies advanced deep learning architectures, such as temporal convolutional networks or transformers, to sequential, high-dimensional epigenetic data (e.g., methylation) captured before and after an acute myocardial infarction (AMI). By training on these longitudinal epigenetic 'movies' rather than single-timepoint 'snapshots,' the models learn to interpret the complex, rapid remodeling of the epigenome in response to ischemic injury. The core innovation is using these models to predict patient-specific epigenetic trajectories, which can then be linked to short- and long-term clinical outcomes, offering a dynamic biomarker of cardiac stress and recovery potential that static genomics or single-timepoint epigenomics cannot provide.

ideas.title: Epigenetic Stabilizers: A 'Sealant' Approach Inspired by Materials Science
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from materials science, where stabilizing a functional surface state with a protective coating is often more effective than trying to reverse widespread corrosion, this idea proposes a shift from reversing malignant epigenetic marks to stabilizing beneficial ones. The analogous solution involves applying sealants to lock in a material's current, undamaged state. Adapted to epigenetics, the research would focus on developing 'epigenetic stabilizer' drugs. These compounds would function by reinforcing the protein machinery (e.g., reader complexes, chromatin architectural proteins) that maintains desirable epigenetic landscapes, effectively 'sealing' healthy transcriptional programs in place and making them resistant to destabilizing signals that drive disease.

ideas.title: Adapting Archaeological Stratigraphy and Provenance Methods for Cancer Epigenetic Timeline Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea adapts methods from archaeology to solve the problem of linking longitudinal lifestyle factors to cancer epigenetics. In archaeology, stratigraphy and provenance analysis are used to reconstruct timelines of human activity from layered deposits and trace artifacts to their source. The analogous solution involves systematic, longitudinal core sampling and meticulous context recording. Adapted to cancer research, this means treating serial patient biospecimens (e.g., pre-cancer, tumor, post-treatment) as a stratigraphic sequence. By applying core sampling logic to longitudinal bio-specimen banking and adapting provenance-tracking to molecular exposure signatures (e.g., specific DNA methylation patterns from known carcinogens), researchers can reconstruct a causal, temporal map of how lifestyle 'exposure events' become layered into the tumor's epigenetic landscape over time.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Sensor Network Principles for Epigenomic Screening Integration
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by drawing an analogy from geophysics, specifically the solution for large-scale seismic monitoring. The analogous field is Geophysics/Seismic Monitoring, where the problem of detecting rare, critical events (earthquakes) across a vast population (a tectonic region) is solved by integrating data from dense, distributed networks of heterogeneous sensors. A central processing architecture fuses this multi-modal data in real-time, using algorithms to distinguish background noise from precursor signals. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to treat diverse epigenetic biomarkers (e.g., cell-free DNA methylation patterns from different tissues) as a distributed sensor network within the bloodstream. The adapted idea is to develop a central computational framework that integrates this multi-source epigenetic data with traditional screening results (like imaging or fecal tests). The system would use advanced machine learning models, trained on large cohorts, to distinguish cancer-associated epigenetic 'signals' from the background 'noise' of age-related or benign epigenetic variation. This creates a scalable, multi-analyte analysis platform for population cancer screening, improving early detection accuracy by synthesizing complementary data streams, much like a seismic network synthesizes data to predict earthquakes.

ideas.title: Methylation as a System Monitor, Not a Crystal Ball
ideas.description: This idea inverts the predictive utility assumption by drawing an analogy from mechanical engineering's predictive maintenance. In that field, vibration and oil debris signals are powerful diagnostic and monitoring tools for current system health and degradation, not primary predictors of a machine's inherent lifespan. Adapting this solution, we propose to treat DNA methylation marks similarly: not as primary predictive biomarkers of future disease, but as diagnostic tools for current cellular state and monitoring tools for ongoing pathological processes. Research would focus on developing methylation signatures as real-time 'molecular wear' monitors—creating dashboards to diagnose active cellular stress (e.g., inflammation, senescence) and track intervention efficacy, thereby reframing their utility from long-term fortune-telling to near-term physiological assessment.

ideas.title: Adapting Fragment-Based Lead Discovery from GPCR research for epigenetic reader probes
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using selective chemical probes, akin to kinase inhibitors, to dissect epigenetic reader protein roles. Drawing an analogy from GPCR pharmacology, where fragment-based lead discovery (FBLD) was adapted to overcome challenges of shallow binding sites and target homology to create subtype-selective ligands. The adapted idea is to apply this FBLD pipeline to epigenetic reader domains. This involves screening fragment libraries biophysically, using structural biology to guide optimization, and developing potent, selective chemical probes that can functionally interrogate specific reader proteins in epigenetic complexes and pathways, moving beyond the limitations of existing broad-spectrum inhibitors.

ideas.title: Adapting Compressed Sensing from Signal Processing to Identify a Minimal, Diagnostic Epigenetic Code
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of compressed sensing from signal processing to the challenge of identifying a minimal epigenetic panel for point-of-care diagnostics. In compressed sensing, a complete signal is accurately reconstructed from a small number of random measurements by leveraging the signal's sparsity. Analogously, we propose treating the disease-specific epigenetic state as a high-dimensional but sparse 'signal' (where only a fraction of loci are informatively modified). The research would involve designing a strategy to identify a very small, fixed panel of randomly selected or optimally chosen epigenetic markers. A tailored reconstruction algorithm, trained on known epigenetic sparsity patterns in health and disease, would then use the data from this minimal panel to infer a comprehensive diagnostic signature. This approach theoretically defines the lower bound of required measurements for robust diagnosis, enabling the design of ultra-simplified assays for rapid, low-cost platforms without sacrificing diagnostic accuracy.

ideas.title: Transferring Convolutional Neural Network Architectures from Computer Vision to Epigenomic Signal Analysis
ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the challenge of interpreting complex epigenetic patterns by drawing an analogy from computer vision. In that field, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) successfully automated feature extraction from raw 2D image pixels by learning hierarchical spatial patterns. We adapt this solution to epigenomics by conceptualizing sequential data—like signals for histone modifications, transcription factor binding, or DNA accessibility across genomic coordinates—as a 1D 'epigenomic image.' A 1D CNN architecture can be applied, where convolutional filters scan along the genome to detect local, recurring epigenetic signatures or combinatorial codes, independent of their exact genomic position. These automatically learned, high-level features replace manual quantification of peaks or windows and serve as input for deep learning models to predict outcomes such as gene expression, cell fate, or disease states, effectively transferring a proven methodological framework from image analysis to genomic signal interpretation.

ideas.title: Context-Adaptive, Fail-Safe Epigenetic Panel with Guardrails for Point-of-Care
ideas.description: This research designs a minimal epigenetic panel not as a single static set, but as a core universal module validated for stability across diverse global populations, augmented by adaptive satellite modules for specific contexts like common co-morbidities. The system incorporates intrinsic technical and biological control markers to create a 'fail-safe' mechanism, where the assay itself can detect and flag samples with insufficient quality or confounding factors before issuing a diagnostic call. This ensures robust performance on rapid, low-cost platforms by making reliability assessment a core output, gracefully degrading to an 'inconclusive' result rather than a misleading one.

ideas.title: A Federated, Harmonized Framework for Multi-Ancestry Methylation Biomarker Discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from multi-messenger astronomy, where data from globally distributed, heterogeneous instruments are federated to study universal phenomena. The analogous problem in biomarker research is validating and discovering methylation signals across diverse, large-scale population cohorts with differing array platforms, lab protocols, and ancestral backgrounds. The adapted solution is to create a federated, harmonized analysis framework for multi-ancestry cohorts. This framework would implement standardized pre-processing and analysis pipelines run locally at each cohort site, outputting summary statistics. A central meta-analysis layer would then integrate these results using advanced methods that model and correct for between-cohort technical and population stratification biases, enabling robust cross-ancestry biomarker validation and discovery without requiring raw data sharing, thus scaling the solution both in cohort size and diversity.

ideas.title: Systematic cis-meQTL Discovery and Multi-Ancestry Mendelian Randomization for Validated CpGs
ideas.description: This redesigned approach begins with a dedicated cis-meQTL mapping study in large, diverse cohorts to identify and validate strong, specific genetic instruments for the target CpGs, prioritizing those with clear biological plausibility. It then performs Mendelian randomization within a framework that explicitly tests for and accounts for horizontal pleiotropy using a suite of sensitivity methods. The analysis is replicated across multiple ancestral populations to assess the consistency of causal estimates. Finally, a triangulation step integrates the MR results with evidence from other study designs to strengthen causal inference.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for Causal Variant Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology from seismic tomography in geophysics to the problem of testing if methylation and disease share causal variants. In seismology, tomography solves an inverse problem using data from multiple sensors to colocalize an event source with subsurface features. The analogous solution is adapted by treating genetic associations as events and molecular QTL data as sensor networks. The resulting research idea is to develop a novel colocalization method that uses a Bayesian inverse modeling framework. This framework would integrate fine-mapped genetic signals for both methylation quantitative trait loci (meQTLs) and disease risk, along with prior information about regulatory networks, to probabilistically infer a shared causal variant and its position within a functional genomic structure, moving beyond simple statistical co-occurrence.

ideas.title: Causal Triangulation Cohort with Multi-Modal Pre-Onset Phenotyping
ideas.description: This design establishes a prospective, deeply phenotyped cohort of individuals at high risk for a target disease, initiating observation well before any clinical onset. It employs a causal triangulation framework, applying multiple complementary analytical methods (e.g., longitudinal within-person models, Mendelian randomization, and negative control analyses) to the same rich dataset to test specific causal hypotheses. The core strength is that a putative cause must demonstrate a consistent signal across several methods, each with different and non-overlapping sources of bias, to be accepted. This approach rigorously separates causes from early consequences by demanding convergent evidence before and during the transition from health to disease.

ideas.title: Adapting Instrumental Variable Methods from Econometrics for Mendelian Randomization on CpGs
ideas.description: This idea expands the original Mendelian randomization plan by drawing a direct analogy from econometric instrumental variable analysis. In econometrics, establishing causality for an exposure (like education) on an outcome (like income) requires finding a valid instrument (e.g., policy changes in compulsory schooling laws) and rigorously testing the core assumptions of relevance, exogeneity, and the exclusion restriction. We adapt this solution to the genomic context. For the four validated CpGs, once genetic instruments (typically cis-acting methylation quantitative trait loci, or meQTLs) are identified, we implement a structured, assumption-testing protocol. This includes verifying instrument strength, conducting extensive sensitivity analyses for horizontal pleiotropy (the genetic analogue of violating the exclusion restriction), and utilizing a two-sample MR framework with independent GWAS summary statistics for the CpGs and the disease outcome of interest. This adapted approach ensures a more robust and defensible causal inference for the epigenetic markers, directly mirroring the methodological rigor applied in econometric causal studies.

ideas.title: Temporal Stratification in Longitudinal Data for Causal Inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'relative chronology' method from historical linguistics to challenge the assumption that a case-control snapshot can distinguish cause from consequence. In linguistics, to determine if Word A caused Word B or vice versa, scholars analyze stratified historical records to see which form appears first and trace its evolution through time layers. Adapted to epidemiology, this involves taking longitudinal cohort data and, for each individual who develops the disease, defining multiple pre-onset time windows (e.g., distant, intermediate, proximate). The exposure, biomarker, or behavioral profiles are compared across these stratified 'case epochs' and against matched controls who never develop the disease. Factors that are elevated even in the distant pre-onset strata are strong causal candidates, while those that only rise in proximate strata are likely secondary consequences of the disease process itself. This temporal stratification within the case group provides a more granular test of causal sequence than standard longitudinal designs.
